🤔 Should we be calling LLMs "reasoning" models?

Niya Stoimenova, who holds a PhD in AI reasoning, says the answer is no. In our new Community Spotlight, she explains why the industry's label is misleading. She breaks down deduction, induction, and abduction — and makes a compelling case for what's missing in today’s AI.

And the gap she points to? It may be the very thing that separates smart AI from real AGI. 

👉 This is a must read for anyone researching, building, or applying AI. 
📖 Read the full story below: 

https://lnkd.in/exucyzfz  

#AI #AGI #LLMs

belief: AI is foundational rather than hypothetical.
source_quote: AI isn’t hypothetical. It’s foundational.
source_text:
⚠️ What if we went on an AI strike for just 48 hours?
No search. No logistics. No fraud detection. No diagnostics.

That’s the idea that Nils Beers breaks down in our latest podcast to demonstrate that AI isn’t hypothetical. It’s foundational.

And that’s exactly why the EU’s AI Act matters, and why it has to get this moment right. We’re not regulating an emerging technology anymore. We’re setting the rules for systems our economy and society already depend on.

So are we writing rules that strengthen these systems — or slow them down?

🎧 Listen to the full conversation (https://lnkd.in/eMXWnPE6) and share your thoughts in the comments!

#AIAct #AI #TheKickstartAIpodcast

Will the EU AI Act hold back innovation?

Our new podcast episode is live 🚀 As guest hosts, our CEO Nils Beers and our content strategist Ioanna Lykiardopoulou explore the real-world impact of the EU’s AI Act — and what it could mean for the future of AI in Europe.

They unpack:

belief: Static websites with rich interactivity are the optimal choice for LLM-assisted development.
source_quote: static websites with rich interactivity are the sweet spot for LLM-assisted development
source_text:
🚨 Our new technical blog is live!
 We explore how we used network analysis and LLMs to map the Netherlands’ threat landscape.

As part of our contribution to DenkWerk’s new report “Resilience by Design,” our ML engineer Cascha van Wanrooij built a node analysis tool to uncover how national threats interconnect, amplify each other, and where intervention can have the greatest impact.

In this post, we dive into:
🔹 Using LLMs to mine causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents
🔹 Applying centrality metrics to find the most influential risks
🔹 Creating an interactive graph for exploring systemic threats
🔹 Tacking the statistical challenge of rare events 
🔹 Why static websites with rich interactivity are the sweet spot for LLM-assisted development  

💡 Tip: Don’t underestimate smaller LLMs like Gemini 2.5 Flash — they’re fast, efficient, and very capable for this kind of semantic extraction work. 

👉 Read the full blog:
 https://lnkd.in/eBCG7BF3

belief: To unlock AI’s potential, organisations must stop treating it like another IT upgrade and instead rethink how they work and operate.
source_quote: To unlock AI’s potential, we have to stop treating it like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate.
source_text:
💡 To unlock AI’s potential, we have to stop treating it like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate.

As Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, AI is too big to comprehend and too different in nature from what we’re used to. And that is why so many organizations struggle to turn potential into impact.

They’re blocked by misaligned incentives. They keep approaching AI as a technology rollout — when in reality, it’s a shift in mindset. It’s not about hiring more developers or running more pilots. Nor about using traditional metrics to measure success.

👉 It’s about the people who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it. That’s exactly what we do at KickstartAI.

#AI #AIadoptionNetherlands #KickstartAI

belief: Human intelligence is fundamentally different from AI, and that difference matters in practice.
source_quote: what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice
source_text:
🧠 The smartest thing humans do? Ignore most of the data.

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer, Cascha van Wanrooij, and Paul Kuyer explore what sets human intelligence apart from AI — and why that difference matters in practice.

AI learns by consuming massive amounts of data. But humans evolved to ignore most information, and act on the small fraction that matters the most.

The contrast goes beyond how we process information. It also shapes what we prioritize, remember, and understand, which influences how we behave. The AI systems we’re currently using have different foundations:
🔎  Current AI architectures don't have an integrated, persistent (and autobiographical) long-term memory
⚙️ Current models operate without intent or self-awareness
📉 But they’re still being used to generate decisions and influence at scale

So what kind of guardrails do we need when deploying systems that lack the constraints and instincts that shaped human cognition?

#TheKickstartAIPodcast #AI #ArtificialIntelligence

belief: Neural networks are powerful and can outperform humans in specific tasks.
source_quote: "neural networks are powerful. They outperform us in specific tasks."
source_text:
🧠 Is our brain a biological computer?

In our latest podcast, Evertjan Peer and Cascha van Wanrooij sit down with Paul Kuyer to unpack a core analogy that sits at the heart of the AGI debate: If the human brain is a biological computer, why can’t a digital computer eventually do all the same things and reach superhuman levels?

While the premise is plausible, the logic behind it also risks being self-reinforcing — assuming the thing it needs to prove. 

Yes, neural networks are powerful. They outperform us in specific tasks. But, at the moment at least, the technology lacks the essential traits that define human cognition: consciousness, wants, and desires.

💬 Are we anthropomorphizing models just because they speak our language? Or do the similarities between brains and machines run deeper than we’re comfortable admitting? And if they do, what kind of caution does this call for? 

👉 Listen to the full discussion here:https://lnkd.in/eFGHnCyE

#TheKickstartAIpodcast #AGI #AI

belief: Organizations need to move from traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs to focusing on the people who are ready to reimagine what’s possible and act on it.
source_quote: We need to move from traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs, to focusing on the people within organizations who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it.
source_text:
AI holds a €85bn potential for the Dutch economy, but as Sophia Claire Zitman pointed out at TNW Conference, adoption is lagging.

It’s not about the technology, talent, or compute. It’s that AI is still stuck in the "potential" phase — too abstract, too big, too misaligned with how organizations actually work.

To unlock its impact, we have to stop treating AI like another IT upgrade. We have to rethink how we work and operate. We need to move from traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs, to focusing on the people within organizations who are ready to reimagine what’s possible — and act on it.

In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
✈️  KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%
🛍️ bol improved parcel delivery efficiency by 6%
🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations increasing safety 

👉 The potential is clear. The time to move from exploration to execution is now.

#KicstartAI #AIinNetherlands #AIadoption

belief: Adoption of AI is not a choice for businesses and government if they want to remain competitive.
source_quote: “Adoption of AI is not a choice. The opportunity is now if we want to compete.
source_text:
Last Friday, we met with MPs Barbara Kathmann (PvdA/GL), Martijn Buijsse(VVD), and Jan Valize (PVV) to share what we’re doing at KickstartAI.

Together with our partners — Ahold Delhaize, bol, ING, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, and Nederlandse Spoorwegen — we highlighted how we’re turning AI into real-world solutions.

From reducing food waste at KLM, to enhancing financial inclusion at ING, improving station safety with NS, and enabling targeted customer experiences at Bol — we jointly focused on concrete, high-impact cases of AI in action.

As Nils Beers, CEO of KickstartAI, said:
“Adoption of AI is not a choice. The opportunity is now if we want to compete. So we need to get to work on actually using AI in our businesses and government.”

We’re encouraged by the support from the MPs and look forward to building momentum for a more positive and actionable AI narrative in the Netherlands.

#AIadoptionNetherlands #AI #AIforgood #AIpolicy

belief: Interconnectedness is a crucial third dimension in threat analysis beyond traditional probability and impact models.
source_quote: moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension
source_text:
Great news! 🚀 We collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. (Link to our tool: https://lnkd.in/dqej7mDQ)

👉 Read the full report here: https://lnkd.in/d7ABqq7f

#ResilienceByDesign #AIforGood #KickstartAI

belief: Today’s threats are deeply intertwined rather than isolated.
source_quote: today, threats are deeply intertwined
source_text:
Great news! 🚀 We collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining how the Netherlands can better prepare for an increasingly interconnected threat landscape.

🔎 The challenge: 
Traditional threat analysis treats risks in isolation. But today, threats are deeply intertwined. A cyberattack can disrupt supply chains. Climate events can ripple into economic shocks. As COVID showed, health crises can spark nationwide consequences.

💡 Our contribution: 
Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, we developed a node analysis tool that maps how threats connect and amplify each other.

We used LLMs to extract causal relationships from thousands of government and research documents — creating a semantic network of interlinked national threats. The result: an interactive graph that helps policymakers explore these complex threat relationships visually, moving beyond traditional "probability × impact" models to include interconnectedness as a crucial third dimension. (Link to our tool: https://lnkd.in/dqej7mDQ)

👉 Read the full report here: https://lnkd.in/d7ABqq7f

#ResilienceByDesign #AIforGood #KickstartAI

belief: AI is not about automating things or digital transformation, but about rewriting, reshaping, and rethinking the way you work.
source_quote: “AI isn’t digital transformation. It's not about automating things. It is about rewriting, reshaping, and rethinking the way you work.”
source_text:
Yesterday at TNW Conference, our very own Sophia Claire Zitman, addressed why AI adoption is lagging despite its €85bn potential for the Dutch economy. The problem isn't the tech or talent; it's that AI is too big to fully grasp.

As Sophia said: “AI isn’t digital transformation. It's not about automating things. It is about rewriting, reshaping, and rethinking the way you work.” 

To realize its potential, we must go beyond traditional metrics like OKRs and KPIs. They simply don’t work. The key is finding the entrepreneurial spirits and change-makers within your organizations — and enabling them to break the mould.  

⚙️ In our projects, this approach has led to real results:
🥗 KLM Royal Dutch Airlines cut food waste by 65%
📦 bol improved parcel delivery efficiency by 6%
🚉 Nederlandse Spoorwegen train stations became safer

Let's stop forcing AI into old frameworks — and embrace the change it brings. 

#KickstartAI #TNWConference2025 #AIadoptionNetherlands

belief: Turning complex AI policy into practical tools is an important and necessary activity.
source_quote: turn complex policy into practical tools
source_text:
The EU AI act is reshaping how we build, deploy, and oversee AI systems — but are you ready for it? 

On July 3rd, join us for a crucial meetup that will help turn complex policy into practical tools. Together with BigData Republic, we’re bringing the AI Act down to earth and into your day-to-day work. 

What to expect: 

🔸 Kaan K. and Cas Mevissen (Policy Officers on AI, Dutch Ministries of Justice and Economic Affairs) will break down the core elements of the AI Act and present the government's rollout plans. 

🔸 Jurriaan Parie (co-founder of Algorithm Audit) will share real-world insights from public audits of machine learning systems. 

🔸 Rens van Dongen (AI Officer at Nederlandse Spoorwegen) will discuss how NS is implementing an internal AI Management System (AIMS) to embed security, ethics, and future-readiness into their AI practice. 

Whether you're an engineer writing code, a strategist defining AI policy, or a compliance professional, this meetup is your essential opportunity to get informed, ask questions, and prepare for what's next.

👉 Interested? Register below!

#EUAIAct #AImeetup #AIinNetherlands

belief: With the right tools and strategy, small language models can deliver big results faster and cheaper.
source_quote: With the right tools and strategy small models can deliver big results — faster and cheaper.
source_text:
At yesterday’s KickstartAI x distil labs workshop, we rolled up our sleeves to explore how fine-tuning SLMs can unlock powerful and cost-effective RAG systems.

🚀 The result? A packed room, lively discussion, and a hands-on look at how to make small models punch above their weight.

💡 Our takeaway: With the right tools and strategy small models can deliver big results — faster and cheaper.

Special thanks to Jacek Golebiowski and Selim Nowicki from diistil labs for leading us through:

🔹 The full SLM fine-tuning pipeline — from lightweight dataset prep to RAG deployment
🔹 A live demo on adapting compact models with just a few examples
🔹 Case studies showing how this approach cuts costs and dev time without sacrificing performance
🔹 A look at the distil labs platform, which simplifies the development and deployment of specialized models

💬 We’d love to hear your takeaways or follow-up thoughts in the comments!

#KickstartAIcommunity #AIevents #SLMs

belief: AI should be used to drive measurable social impact.
source_quote: Drive measurable social impact
source_text:
🚀 Applications for the National AI Challenge 2025 are open!

You don’t need a finished plan — just a real-world challenge and the ambition to make a difference. If you believe AI could be part of the solution, we’ll help you find out how.

Together with AIC4NL, we’re inviting corporates, public institutions, and purpose-driven organizations to submit the challenges that matter most. We’ll collaborate with selected teams to explore, prototype, and apply AI — step by step.

This is your chance to:
✅ Develop a working AI solution
✅ Drive measurable social impact
✅ Access expert support and a powerful AI network
✅ Boost your visibility as a leader in responsible innovation
 
Join a growing ecosystem shaping the future of AI in the Netherlands
Following a successful 2024 edition, we’ll select two winners this year to launch two new AI projects.

🗓️ Application deadline: July 25, 2025
💻  Project phase starts: September 2025

 Want to learn more?
 👉 Join our Q&A session on June 17 (15:00-16:00)
 📩 Register by emailing iris.visser@kickstart.ai

 🔗 Apply now or read more via the link below:
https://lnkd.in/etNzMRHD

belief: Leadership in AI will come from those who take action and responsibility rather than from debate alone.
source_quote: We believe leadership in AI will come from those who take action and responsibility.
source_text:
The Dutch government has fallen. But AI progress can’t wait.

Political views aside — this government shouldn't have collapsed. Not now. The challenges we face are too big and too urgent. But it’s also a clear signal: we can’t wait for The Hague alone to solve them.

The future won’t be shaped in debate rooms — it will be shaped by action. By people who dare to do.

At KickstartAI, we build real-world AI solutions every day that make the Netherlands smarter, fairer, and more human. We believe leadership in AI will come from those who take action and responsibility.

No more talk. No more delays.

It’s time to:
✅ Accelerate real AI adoption in Dutch businesses and institutions
✅ Show that AI delivers results and social impact

We’re joining forces with those who believe the Netherlands should lead in applied AI — not just talk about it. And in doing so, we aim to give future policymakers something solid to build on: real lessons to take into manifestos, election debates, and a new coalition agreement.

Let’s come together. And make it happen.

Ahold Delhaize KLM Royal Dutch Airlines Nederlandse Spoorwegen ING Nederland Ampère bol

#AIinNetherlands #AIadoption

belief: AI is emerging as a key driver in the energy transition.
source_quote: AI is emerging as a key driver in the energy transition
source_text:
AI is reshaping the energy sector ⚡️

Learn how in our latest deep-dive article:

AI is emerging as a key driver in the energy transition, supporting everything from grid management and renewable integration to energy efficiency and predictive system control.

We spoke with four leading experts working at the intersection of AI and energy to understand the current state and future potential.

What you'll learn:
- Why the Netherlands faces unique grid challenges that make AI solutions particularly urgent
- How AI is accelerating grid reliability assessments from hours to seconds
- Real-world applications delivering financial benefits through smart energy scheduling
- The hidden energy costs of AI itself and practical strategies to minimize them
- Future directions, such as  hyper-personalized energy services and foundation models
→ Read our full deep-dive: [link in comments]

Thanks to Yuliya Shapovalova Arno van den Berg Adil Bohoudi Alex Imbrea for sharing your insights 🙏

belief: KickstartAI believes in prioritising concrete action over mere conversation regarding AI.
source_quote: we believe in action — not just conversation
source_text:
At KickstartAI, we believe in action — not just conversation.

That’s why it’s great to hear voices like Michiel Bakker and Jelle Prins sounding the wake-up call: true AI adoption in the Netherlands requires practical, immediate steps. 

One key move is swiftly applying AI in business in the sectors where the Netherlands has the most to gain. Think of the manufacturing industry or logistics.

Let’s build a coalition of doing!
✅ By focusing on adoption and application of AI in practice.
✅ By getting the doers in AI to the table: techies, AI founders, engineers.
✅ By creating policies that enable AI adoption — through talent development, access to computing power, and room for experimentation and concrete action.

Let’s make it happen. 

#AIinNetherlands #AIadoptionNetherlands

belief: Artificial intelligence creates more value in more complex environments when it is paired with the right domain expertise.
source_quote: The more complex the environment, the more value AI can unlock — when paired with the right domain expertise.
source_text:
At yesterday’s KickstartAI x Boskalis workshop, we explored how machine learning and simulation models can optimize the logistics of soil supply and demand — to improve dike construction. 

🧠 The result? Our community members worked together and came up with five different ideas on how to make this happen!

💡 Our takeaway: The more complex the environment, the more value AI can unlock — when paired with the right domain expertise.

A big thank you to Rohit Gupta, PhD for his fascinating talk on Reinforcement Learning and how to apply it in practice!

Stay tuned for our upcoming events!

#KickstartAI #AIcommunityNetherlands #AIinEnergy

belief: Alternative cloud models should prioritize effectiveness and sustainability over conventional notions of efficiency in IT infrastructure.
source_quote: advocated for alternative cloud models that prioritize effectiveness and sustainability
source_text:
AI is transforming the world, but at what cost? During our Meetup yesterday in Amsterdam, we explored ways to optimize AI’s energy use. 

David Kohnstamm (founder and CSO at leafcloud), challenged the conventional thinking around efficiency in IT infrastructure, and advocated for alternative cloud models that prioritize effectiveness and sustainability. 

Wilco Burggraaf (Principal Lead at HighTech Innovators) delved into practical methods on how to track and manage AI’s energy footprint — using tools like the Software Carbon Intensity (SCI) formula and built-in energy tracking for AI workloads on Kubernetes.

Robert Keus (founder of GreenPT) and Cas Burggraaf (CTO at GreenPT) shared how they’ve set up a scalable (and green) AI platform. They also stressed a key takeaway: educating users to prompt better is essential to cutting energy consumption. 

Thanks to everyone who joined us yesterday, and we look forward to seeing you at the next Meetup in June! 

#KickstartAI #AIcommunityNetherlands #SustainableAI

belief: Applied AI should be designed for real needs, shaped by people, and built for impact.
source_quote: This is how we believe applied AI should be done — designed for real needs, shaped by people, and built for impact.
source_text:
🧠 How do you identify an AI use case and turn it into a solution that actually works — in the real world, with real people, under real pressure?

In this video, we take you behind the scenes of our project with Erasmus MC, where we are building an AI tool to help predict ER patient inflow, providing staff with better foresight and helping the hospital manage capacity more effectively.

But the real story isn’t just about the model.
It’s about the process:
🔍 Understanding the real operational need
🗣️ Walking the floor and talking to users
🧩 Mapping where AI fits into existing workflows
📍 Designing for adoption from day one

This is how we believe applied AI should be done — designed for real needs, shaped by people, and built for impact.

A big thank you to Sophia Claire Zitman, Margrietha H. (Greet) Vink, Marjolein Kremers, Nicky Hekster for their insights!

Watch the full breakdown of practical steps and advice in the video below 👇 

#KickstartAI #ErasmusMC #AIimplementation

belief: Advancements in machine learning show that relatively small models can generate diverse, physically plausible synthetic data, scenarios, and entire worlds on demand.
source_quote: Advancements in machine learning show that even relatively small models (like the 7B-parameter Cosmos) can generate diverse, physically plausible synthetic data, scenarios, and even entire worlds on demand.
source_text:
We took Nvidia’s Cosmos model for a spin and created a realistic driving simulation in Amsterdam 🚘 

Advancements in machine learning show that even relatively small models (like the 7B-parameter Cosmos) can generate diverse, physically plausible synthetic data, scenarios, and even entire worlds on demand.

This capability enables developers to test AI systems across billions of virtual kilometers and countless scenarios — leading to safer, more capable AI for real-world applications like driving.

What's your take on this? Drop a comment and join the discussion 👇 

Find the full podcast, featuring Evertjan Peer and Cascha V., here: https://lnkd.in/dhj5qyFE

belief: The development of more predictive simulators, more robust AI perception models, and more compute-efficient simulation methods is a key convergence driving progress toward truly smart cities.
source_quote: we’re now at the convergence point of three key advancements
source_text:
Do you think we’re closer than ever to living in truly smart cities? 

As Evertjan Peer and Cascha V. discussed on our latest podcast, we’re now at the convergence point of three key advancements: 

➡️ More predictive simulators that better mirror real-world outcomes. 
➡️ More robust AI perception models (especially for vision) that can handle the differences between simulation and reality.
➡️ More compute-efficient methods for running large-scale simulations and generating realistic training data.

belief: Artificial intelligence should be used to solve some of the world’s most pressing societal challenges and improve people’s lives.
source_quote: use AI to solve some of the world’s most pressing societal challenges
source_text:
🗣️ Applications for this year’s National AI Challenge are open!

Together with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL, we’re calling on organizations looking to use AI to solve some of the world’s most pressing societal challenges. This is your chance to create solutions that drive real change and improve people’s lives!

Following a successful 2024 edition, this year, we’ll select two winners and work with them on two separate projects.

💡 Who can apply? 
Private, public, or social organisations that are (or interested in becoming) members of AIC4NL.

Selection criteria: 
➡️ Relevance and urgency of the problem
➡️ Available support by your organisation
➡️ Feasibility
➡️ Impact and scalability
➡️ Willingness to share the results

Why join? 
✅ Develop a working AI solution for your organization together with experts
✅ Make social impact 
✅ Gain access to technical expertise, project support, and a powerful network
✅ Increase the visibility of your organization as an AI pioneer 
✅ Become part of an inspiring ecosystem where collaboration is key 

🗓️ Deadline for applications: July 25, 2025

Interested in more information or the application form? Follow the link below: https://lnkd.in/etNzMRHD

#NationalAIChallenge #AI #AIinNetherlands

belief: Thoroughly understanding and defining the problem you aim to solve is essential to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan for applying AI in healthcare operations.
source_quote: Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve: This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.
source_text:
At the Dutch AI Congress this week, we had the opportunity to share key insights on how to develop and implement AI solutions in healthcare organizations.

For the past seven months, we’ve been working closely with Erasmus MC to predict patient inflow at the emergency department — a step toward improving efficiency, capacity management, and staffing.

Our Tech Lead, Evertjan P., took the stage to talk about what it really takes to apply AI in hospital operational processes. Here are three of the most important lessons we've learned so far:

💡 Thoroughly understand and define the problem you’re aiming to solve:
This is key to creating a specific goal and a concrete plan.

💡 Assess feasibility: 
What data sources are available, and what level of performance can an AI model achieve based on these? How can we use these AI-driven insights in day-to-day practice?

💡 Determine the processes for tech integration and user adoption:
Ask: What’s the IT infrastructure like? How will the AI tool be presented to end-users? How should it be used in practice?

We’re proud to be part of the AI movement in the Netherlands — and excited for what’s next. 🧡 

A big shoutout to the AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL for organizing this inspiring congress!

#AI #HealthcareAI #HealthcareInnovation

belief: Accelerating AI adoption in the Netherlands is an important goal for the organisation.
source_quote: to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands
source_text:
Very proud to announce that we once again joined forces with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. Frans Muller, CEO of Ahold Delhaize, one of the founding partner companies of KickstartAI, today unveiled the second edition of the National AI Challenge, the flagship initiative of our renewed collaboration. 

The National AI Challenge was first introduced by KickstartAI and AI Coalition 4 NL in 2024, aiming to solve  concrete challenges in businesses and social organizations with artificial intelligence. The winner of the first edition, Erasmus MC, has worked with KickstartAI over the past year to improve efficiency and quality of care in the Emergency Department using AI technology.

This year’s National AI Challenge will start at the end of April and will select not one, but two use cases.

Read more about our collaboration in our press release https://lnkd.in/d-MnC-Vc  and stay tuned for more details about how to participate in the Challenge!

Pieter Jeekel Nils Beers Tjarda Voorneman Frans Muller Keely Richardson Klaas Wagenaar

belief: KickstartAI assumes that individual practitioners are the primary drivers of meaningful progress in AI, more so than institutions or abstract technological trends.
source_quote: we believe that meaningful progress starts with individuals — engineers, researchers, founders, and practitioners — who are applying AI in bold, practical ways
inference_reasoning: By framing progress as starting with individuals and spotlighting them rather than organisations or technologies, the text presupposes a people-centric view of how change in AI happens.
source_text:
====================================================================================================
URL: https://kickstart.ai/news/community-spotlight-lennard-kooy-on-building-llevarage-product-market-fit-and-ai-automation
====================================================================================================

=== Community Spotlight: Lennard Kooy ===

--- Building Lleverage, AI automation, and the search for product-market fit ---

Every month, we spotlight a member of our community. At KickstartAI, we believe that meaningful progress starts with individuals — engineers, researchers, founders, and practitioners — who are applying AI in bold, practical ways. Through our Community Spotlight series, we celebrate the people shaping the future of AI in the Netherlands: their work, their challenges, and the lessons they’re learning along the way.

This month, we’re featuring Lennard Kooy, co-founder and CEO of Lleverage — a startup making AI-powered automation accessible to everyone. From building with natural language to navigating the search for product-market fit, this is his story.

It’s been less than a year since Lennard Kooy co-founded Lleverage — an end-to-end AI platform that automates complex business processes without the need for developers. A serial founder, Lennard has built four SaaS startups to date, but Lleverage is the first one that has a 100% AI DNA.

“In all honesty, I think that if you're starting a SaaS company now and it’s not an AI company, there has to be a very good reason for it,” Lennard says.

He believes that, in five years time, 80% to 90% of what developers currently write in code will be created using natural language — making AI a fundamental technology for future SaaS startups.

=== The birth of Lleverage ===

The idea behind Lleverage came from firsthand experience. At his previous SaaS company in marketing technology, Storyteq, Lennard was already experimenting with generative AI. It felt like a natural fit: GenAI excels at language and visuals — the backbone of ads.

But when it came to actually integrating this technology into the product, Lennard and Badr Eddial (at the time, Storyteq’s co-founder and CTO) ran into a problem. Their team of developers struggled with the non-deterministic nature of AI and new concepts like RAG pipelines and vector databases.

“When we left Storyteq, we thought this was quite an interesting problem,” Lennard says. “So we started out with making a platform to bridge the gap between traditional developer skills and the power of AI.”

While Llevarge was born to simplify AI workloads for developers, the focus quickly expanded to a business audience, such as product owners and business experts.

The pivot followed early conversations with customers. Lennard and his team realized that, by automating business processes using just natural language, Leverage could directly cater to non-technical teams in need of technical solutions. The traction in their deal pipeline was significantly quicker with COOs and CEOs compared to CTOs, supporting this change in direction.

=== Under the hood ===

The first thing that Lleverage built was a workflow engine to facilitate data flow in automations. The team decided from the beginning to differentiate the company from chat interfaces like ChatGPT or Gemini and focus on the data processing pipelines rather than the end-user interface.

Lennard explains that the startup’s core technology is the workflow engine — primarily built with JavaScript and powered by Natural Language Processing (NLP).  Underneath this engine, there are multiple building blocks, from various AI models to vector databases like Weviate.

In essence, Lleverage offers “describe-and-build” AI workflow solutions. This is what it calls “vibe automation.”

belief: Lleverage assumes that future competitive advantage in SaaS will depend on enabling non-technical business users to build and automate with AI using natural language rather than code.
source_quote: automates complex business processes without the need for developers
inference_reasoning: The repeated emphasis on ‘without the need for developers’ and serving COOs/CEOs implies a strategic belief that empowering non-technical users via natural language interfaces will be key to winning in the SaaS market.
source_text:

belief: Early-stage startups must eventually commit to a clear go-to-market model rather than sustaining a hybrid approach for long.
source_quote: “We can’t maintain this position for a long time, because it defocuses a small company like us.”
inference_reasoning: Describing the in-between state as unsustainable and defocusing for a small company implies a broader strategic belief that early-stage startups need a single, clearly chosen motion instead of a prolonged hybrid model.
source_text:
Such an architecture doesn’t come without its challenges, according to Lennard. The complexity and volume of data processing in user-defined pipelines create significant technical demands. Behind the scenes, Lleverage has to ensure data integrity, handle retries for external API failures, and manage the non-deterministic nature of AI outputs — all while keeping the experience smooth for the user.

But the most complex problem Lleverage has faced so far isn’t a technical one.

=== The greatest early-stage challenge: finding product-market fit ===

“The biggest challenge for a company in our stage is finding the right product-market fit,” Lennard says. “It’s an iffy concept.”

“When do you feel that your product metrics are giving you the signals that your positioning is right for you? Or when do you think that there’s not enough real traction and you need to keep changing and do another iteration on the product or the business proposition?”

“It’s a constant struggle,” he adds.

Lennard also stresses that the search for product-market fit influences every part of the business: from the business model and the pricing strategy to the type of talent needed and the overall go-to-market motion.

“When transitioning to a sales-led enterprise play, for example, you need to think of a different organizational structure, with a focus on customer success, solutions engineers, and dedicated account executives,” he says.

He compares this to a product-led growth strategy, where the focus shifts towards growth marketing and community management.

Lleverage itself is now in-between the sales-led and the product-led growth motion.

“We’re consciously not making a decision right now,” Lennard explains. “But we’re going to make one in the following months. We can’t maintain this position for a long time, because it defocuses a small company like us.”

Besides market fit, Lennard mentions another challenge for early-stage companies: funding. The startup raised €3mn earlier this month, bringing its total investment to €5mn in less than a year. It has managed to avoid the pitfalls of the funding rollercoaster thanks to its seasoned founders who have already built and scaled successful companies.

But for first-time founders, attracting capital — especially in the AI space — is no easy task, Lennard says. “You have to convince VCs about the defensibility of your idea and that you can actually outgrow your competition.”

=== Building a startup is no bed of roses ===

When asked about the biggest lesson he’s learned on his journey with Lleverage, Lennard doesn’t hesitate: “I had forgotten how painful it is to build a startup.”

He cautions prospective founders against romanticizing the concept of a startup. “Make sure you actually want it and talk to people who have done it before or are doing it now.”

At the same time, he advises against inaction. “If you really want it, just go for it, because sitting on the fence for eternity is also something you shouldn’t do.”

Another important lesson for Lennard is figuring out how to handle competition — especially in a space as crowded and fast-moving as AI.

“I don’t open my LinkedIn every day anymore, because every time I do, I see another competitor,” he jokes. He explains that being confronted with competition every day is not only draining, but also distracting.

“You have to find the right balance between listening to those noises and just believe in what you’re building. There’s a lot of value in perseverance.”

=== Growing the Dutch AI ecosystem needs more work ===

Lennard contrasts the Netherlands with France, where active government support, investor focus, and ambitious infrastructure — like the soon-to-launch world’s largest startup campus — are transforming the country into Europe’s AI powerhouse.

“I feel in the last six months we’re making steps, but before that, there wasn’t a lot of noise around the ecosystem,” he says. “I think we can still do more.”

belief: Founders should treat competition as background noise and prioritize focused perseverance over constant competitive monitoring.

inference_reasoning: Referring to competitors as “noise” and emphasizing belief in one’s own product and perseverance implies a value that sustained focus and resilience are more important than reacting continuously to competitors.

Lennard says that initiatives like KickstartAI have a role to play. He joined the community when he attended one of our events earlier this year. “I think that communities can be a big boost to the ecosystem and they’re a great way for people to feel that they are part of something.”

=== Employee Spotlight: Aneela Mujahid ===

Meet Aneela Mujahid, Chief Operating Officer at KickstartAI. With a unique path to the tech industry and a passion for creating strong foundations, Aneela plays a crucial role in empowering KickstartAI’s technical team so they can focus on impact.

Let's dive into her journey and how she helps create an environment where AI talent can thrive.

>>> What's your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I'm the Chief Operating Officer. I’m responsible for  all the non-technical day-to-day operations, from administration to finance and HR. I don't directly manage a team, but I work closely with all team leaders.

>>> Can you tell us about your journey to KickstartAI? <<<

I didn’t follow the “traditional” path to AI. I got my Bachelor's degree in sales and marketing in Denmark. When I moved to the Netherlands, I joined a tech company in the renewables sector — I was always drawn to the tech industry. I quickly became inspired by all the engineers and their approach to building things.

I worked in renewable energy for six years, and then I got the chance to join KickstartAI and be part of the AI adoption in the Netherlands. What drew me in was the opportunity to help build a team from the ground up and be part of the entire journey — something I found really exciting.

I  joined the team as Office Manager. I quickly started handling more and more administrative, HR, and finance responsibilities. So after helping the team grow for a year, I was promoted to COO.

>>> What are you most proud of in your work at KickstartAI? <<<

Building up the team from the beginning and taking on this ever-evolving role. Within two weeks after starting, I had onboarded everyone who started with me. I gradually drafted all the policies, guidelines, and everything that's in place for our operations. I think that's still my proudest achievement at KickstartAI because it laid the foundation for everything that followed. Our technical team focuses on coding and solving problems — not on operations and administration. My role is to create a really strong base that enables everyone to do their best work and achieve their goals. I'm there to support them in getting where they need to go.

>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

Building KickstartAI’s foundation behind the scenes is definitely what drives me. Empowering others also motivates me. Seeing the team thrive and knowing that my support helps my colleagues is very important to me.

Part of this is creating the right office environment and culture, but also making sure we hire the right people who get along and complement each other.

>>> What interested you about working in AI? <<<

To be honest, I ended up in AI by accident! As I like the tech industry in general, I thought this would be an incredible experience and an opportunity to stay in a technical space and work with technical people. I find that my personality matches well with a more introverted technical team. We get along really well!

>>> What do you enjoy most about working at KickstartAI? <<<

I love that my role is so varied — from HR to finance — so every day is different. It keeps things interesting!

belief: Careful hiring for interpersonal fit and complementarity is assumed to be a key mechanism for creating a thriving AI team environment.
source_quote: making sure we hire the right people who get along and complement each other.
inference_reasoning: The text links team thriving and culture to hiring people who get along and complement each other, implying an underlying belief that selecting for relational and complementary qualities is a primary lever for building an effective AI team.

belief: KickstartAI assumes that a fun, socially warm office environment is important for sustaining employee engagement and cultural fit.
source_quote: I enjoy going to the office because it's always fun. We laugh a lot and it's never boring.
inference_reasoning: By framing enjoyment, fun, and lack of boredom as reasons to value the culture and go to the office, the text presupposes that such an environment is a desirable and necessary condition for engagement and fit, not just a nice-to-have.
source_text:
I also value our open and transparent culture. Everyone's friendly with each other, and I enjoy going to the office because it's always fun. We laugh a lot and it's never boring.

We used to organize "lunch and learn" sessions with deep dives for the technical team and more general sessions for our non-technical people who wanted to better understand AI. Last year, we even did a "100 Days of Coding" challenge with the group of non-techies. Every week, we'd sit together for an hour and try coding ourselves to really understand what was happening around us. That was a lot of fun!

>>> What AI tools do you use most in your daily work? <<<

Google Gemini helps me the most, especially when working with contracts. I can quickly search paragraphs without reading through 20-30 pages. I use AI assistants also when writing emails to be more efficient and find the right tone of voice.

I've also started using Venice.ai. It's a collection of models where you can choose between Deep Seek and Llama. I prefer it over ChatGPT now.

>>> What advice would you give to people who want to work in AI or at KickstartAI? <<<

Just be yourself! And be passionate about tech and AI — that's what makes someone a great fit in our team and culture. Also, if you’re looking for diverse projects, we are the place to be — you won’t get bored here. We're actually planning to double our people by the end of the year if we can hire and recruit the technical engineers and project managers we need. The open vacancies are already on our website . We’re definitely expanding the dream team!

>>> Fun fact about yourself? <<<

My way to show I care is feeding people with sweets. So be aware, our office is packed with candy!

💡 Want to learn more about Kickstart AI and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , explore our open positions , or connect with Aneela on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

=== Community Spotlight: Michael Dubbeldam ===

--- Building FlowCube: Inside vision AI for traffic management ---

Every month, we spotlight a member of our community . At KickstartAI, we believe that meaningful progress starts with individuals — engineers, researchers, founders, and practitioners — who are applying AI in bold, practical ways. Through our Community Spotlight series, we celebrate the people shaping the future of AI in the Netherlands: their work, their challenges, and the lessons they’re learning along the way.

This month, we’re featuring Michael Dubbeldam, Domain Architect at Technolution — the company behind FlowCube, a camera-based traffic sensor with embedded deep learning models. From solving real-world mobility challenges in Copenhagen to pioneering vision AI, this is his story.

Michael Dubbeldam started out as a software engineer and, later on, moved into software architecture. He joined Technolution in 2005, where he’s now leading the AI competence group. His interest in AI was first sparked by a traffic management project for the city of Copenhagen in 2017.

“The city wanted a system that could perform as well for cyclists as it did for cars,” Michael recalls. “They needed to evaluate how well the streets were serving cyclists and collect information on how much time cyclists need to go from one side of the city to the other via a particular route.”

At that time, the only way to gather that data was by physically recording videos and then manually analyzing the footage to track individual cyclists.

Naturally, that process took an enormous amount of time. “At that point, we thought: if only there was a system that could do something like that. If only we could use a camera to see what we see.”

belief: KickstartAI assumes that broad, hands-on AI literacy among both technical and non-technical staff is necessary for effective participation in its work and culture.
source_quote: "lunch and learn" sessions... for our non-technical people who wanted to better understand AI... "100 Days of Coding" challenge with the group of non-techies
inference_reasoning: The repeated investment in structured learning and coding challenges for non-technical staff implies a taken-for-granted belief that everyone, not just engineers, should understand and directly experiment with AI to fully participate in the organisation’s activities and culture.
source_text:
I also value our open and transparent culture. Everyone's friendly with each other, and I enjoy going to the office because it's always fun. We laugh a lot and it's never boring.

We used to organize "lunch and learn" sessions with deep dives for the technical team and more general sessions for our non-technical people who wanted to better understand AI. Last year, we even did a "100 Days of Coding" challenge with the group of non-techies. Every week, we'd sit together for an hour and try coding ourselves to really understand what was happening around us. That was a lot of fun!

>>> What AI tools do you use most in your daily work? <<<

Google Gemini helps me the most, especially when working with contracts. I can quickly search paragraphs without reading through 20-30 pages. I use AI assistants also when writing emails to be more efficient and find the right tone of voice.

I've also started using Venice.ai. It's a collection of models where you can choose between Deep Seek and Llama. I prefer it over ChatGPT now.

>>> What advice would you give to people who want to work in AI or at KickstartAI? <<<

Just be yourself! And be passionate about tech and AI — that's what makes someone a great fit in our team and culture. Also, if you’re looking for diverse projects, we are the place to be — you won’t get bored here. We're actually planning to double our people by the end of the year if we can hire and recruit the technical engineers and project managers we need. The open vacancies are already on our website . We’re definitely expanding the dream team!

>>> Fun fact about yourself? <<<

My way to show I care is feeding people with sweets. So be aware, our office is packed with candy!

💡 Want to learn more about Kickstart AI and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , explore our open positions , or connect with Aneela on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

=== Community Spotlight: Michael Dubbeldam ===

--- Building FlowCube: Inside vision AI for traffic management ---

Every month, we spotlight a member of our community . At KickstartAI, we believe that meaningful progress starts with individuals — engineers, researchers, founders, and practitioners — who are applying AI in bold, practical ways. Through our Community Spotlight series, we celebrate the people shaping the future of AI in the Netherlands: their work, their challenges, and the lessons they’re learning along the way.

This month, we’re featuring Michael Dubbeldam, Domain Architect at Technolution — the company behind FlowCube, a camera-based traffic sensor with embedded deep learning models. From solving real-world mobility challenges in Copenhagen to pioneering vision AI, this is his story.

Michael Dubbeldam started out as a software engineer and, later on, moved into software architecture. He joined Technolution in 2005, where he’s now leading the AI competence group. His interest in AI was first sparked by a traffic management project for the city of Copenhagen in 2017.

“The city wanted a system that could perform as well for cyclists as it did for cars,” Michael recalls. “They needed to evaluate how well the streets were serving cyclists and collect information on how much time cyclists need to go from one side of the city to the other via a particular route.”

At that time, the only way to gather that data was by physically recording videos and then manually analyzing the footage to track individual cyclists.

Naturally, that process took an enormous amount of time. “At that point, we thought: if only there was a system that could do something like that. If only we could use a camera to see what we see.”

belief: Preserving individual privacy is a non‑negotiable design constraint for their AI-based traffic sensing solutions.
source_quote: “We don’t track people,” Michael emphasizes. “It’s about statistical patterns
inference_reasoning: By emphatically contrasting tracking people with using only statistical patterns, the text presupposes that privacy protection is an essential requirement that shapes how the system must be built, not just an optional feature.
source_text:
Michael explains the rationale behind that idea.

“Traditional traffic management systems relying on traffic light input provide limited information,” he says. “But as humans, going into the field and seeing what’s happening, we can perceive so much more.”

A camera could potentially replace human eyes, but there was a challenge: enabling a computer to provide and translate all the necessary information.

Michael believed that vision AI could be the answer.

“I thought that with AI, computers can see the same things or understand the same things as humans. So you don’t need to adjust the world to computers; instead, computers can adjust to the human world.”

“That’s why I really got into AI. I wanted to understand how it works, build my own models, and push the boundaries of what’s possible.”

The Copenhagen challenge marked the first vision AI project for Michael and the Technolution team. It also served as the inspiration for a new product journey.

=== FlowCube: from concept to product ===

Michael’s first foray into vision AI gave birth to the concept of  FlowCube —  a camera-based traffic sensor with embedded deep learning models that measure traffic flows of vehicles, bicycles, and pedestrians.

“When we first started, it wasn’t a cube,” he says. Initially, the team created a device with a camera that processed images locally, converting them into data. The system would send information in the form of “I saw a cyclist with a blue coat at this location at this moment,” Michael explains. That way, it could analyze traffic without storing or transmitting any raw images.

“Over time, we designed a nice cube and kept making the system smarter and more efficient,” he says.

It took about two years to reach the minimum viable product stage. “Once you’ve had that, you think that you’re done and can take on the world,” Michael adds. “But as you build, you learn more about what you need to do. We’ve kept refining and improving the system ever since, and I expect it will be even better five years from now.”

Today, FlowCube comes as a box measuring 13x13x13cm. Inside, there’s an inexpensive commodity camera, a computer, an Nvidia processor, and a 4G mobile connection.

=== Building with Siamese models ===

FlowCube captures images and is able to detect objects and people and map their movement in real time.

“We get information like: there’s a cyclist with a blue coat and a bag on the bike at this location and time,” Michael says. The device then converts this type of data into what the team calls a “match code” — a statistical representation of an object’s appearance.

These match codes are sent to a cloud server, where data from multiple FlowCube devices is combined to reconstruct city-wide mobility patterns. This way, the system provides a complete picture of traffic across all road users, while preserving the privacy of individuals.

“We don’t track people,” Michael emphasizes. “It’s about statistical patterns and comparing likelihoods.” For example, that a cyclist at point AI is statistically similar to another one at point B.

To achieve this, the team uses Siamese models — trained not to classify objects, but to assess how alike two inputs are. In this case, not whether two people are both cyclists, but whether they might be the same individual seen in different locations.

That’s useful for what’s called “person re-identification,” where the goal is to determine how much two people resemble each other.

The system is trained using labeled images, so it learns what features actually help distinguish individuals. “For example, it might learn that background or the fact that someone is on two wheels isn’t helpful, but clothing color or a backpack might be.”

belief: AI systems should adapt to existing human environments and practices rather than forcing people or cities to change to accommodate technology.
source_quote: “So you don’t need to adjust the world to computers; instead, computers can adjust to the human world.”
inference_reasoning: The contrast between adjusting the world to computers and computers adjusting to the human world implies a broader stance that technological design ought to fit human contexts instead of requiring humans to reorganize around machines.

belief: Building trustworthy AI systems for traffic management requires not only technical privacy safeguards but also proactive transparency and collaboration with public authorities.
source_quote: “But we also need to involve the cities or municipalities we’re working with in our processes, so they understand our privacy safeguards.”
inference_reasoning: By framing involvement of cities as something they "need" to do so that authorities understand safeguards, the text presupposes that technical measures alone are insufficient for trust and that openness and collaboration are essential components of responsible AI practice.
source_text:
Michael’s team is also working to make the system’s decisions more interpretable. “We’re now exploring how we can name those distinguishing features explicitly — like ‘white sneakers’ or ‘blue coat’ — so the reasoning is understandable not just to machines, but to people too.”

For model training, Technolution creates its own datasets. “For the re-identification models, for example, we have a dataset of approximately 250,000 images and 15,000 identities,” Michael says. “We’ve also needed to adapt our training data to the local context.” For instance, vehicles in the US, include pick-up trucks and yellow school buses — which are uncommon in Europe. In Nordic countries, cyclists typically wear helmets unlike in the Netherlands.

=== Challenges ===

Michael identifies three main challenges in building and developing FlowCube. The first one is hardware: building a device that’s robust, scalable, easy to manufacture, and consistently reliable in the field.

Another difficulty is creating models that generalize well across different environments. Something that works in one place may not work elsewhere without retraining.

Finally, the third major challenge lies in the development of the Siamese models. “Most research on the technology of person or object re-identification works with closed-world benchmarks, where a match is guaranteed. You simply have to find the best one.”

But in real open-world environments, this isn’t the case. “You don’t know if a match exists,” Michael explains.

This gap between closed- versus open-world applications meant that the team couldn’t rely on existing models or benchmarks. “We had to define our models, layers, cost functions, and training to get them to do what we needed them to do.”

=== AI’s role in multimodal traffic management ===

For Michael, the biggest benefit AI brings to traffic management is access to human-level information. Traditional sensors can’t distinguish a bus from a car based on visual cues. “Vision AI can,” he emphasizes.

AI-based sensors can make smarter decisions and optimize the overall traffic management system — for example, by adjusting traffic lights to prioritize buses. This optimization is particularly important for multimodal countries, with a large number of cyclists and pedestrians, like the Netherlands.

Equally important is to comply with regulations like the GDPR in order to increase both customer and public trust.

“Images never leave the device — they are processed on the edge and then discarded. We don’t store identifiable information,” Michael says.

“But we also need to involve the cities or municipalities we’re working with in our processes, so they understand our privacy safeguards.”

Michael joined the KickstartAI community only recently, but already sees value.

“There aren’t many people who are deeply in this field and who are bringing lab work to reality. So it’s good to see how others manage the same things we struggle with. To me, that’s really educational. And I’m reading a lot that I find very interesting.”

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

=== Shield or sword? The role of AI in safety and security ===

By Ioanna Lykiardopoulou

Today, AI plays a critical role in strengthening our safety across diverse domains, from cyber defense to combating financial crime and protecting national security. Yet this same capability also exposes us to serious risks: AI can serve as both a powerful shield and a vicious sword, depending on who wields it.

belief: The organisation assumes that prioritising model accuracy over built-in safety is a widespread and problematic norm in current AI development and deployment.
source_quote: Accuracy trumps safety
inference_reasoning: By framing accuracy as trumping safety and linking this to companies’ reluctance to invest in secure systems, the text presupposes that this priority ordering is common practice and implicitly problematic, not just an isolated observation.
source_text:
This isn’t just about speculative, existential dangers. It’s about tangible, immediate threats stemming from misuse: generative AI tools enabling deepfakes, AI-driven social engineering scams, or autonomous systems deployed with unclear oversight.

This edition explores how AI is being used to reinforce security in cybersecurity, financial fraud detection, threat prediction, and military defense operations. It also looks inward at the field of AI safety: how experts are working to make these systems more robust, explainable, and aligned with human intent.

To better understand the current landscape in the Netherlands, we spoke with four leading experts working at the intersection of AI, safety, and security:

Stjepan Picek , Associate Professor of Digital Security at Radboud University

Stjepan Picek , Associate Professor of Digital Security at Radboud University

Iskander Hannivoort , Founder of Manukai , Investor, and Airline Captain at Transavia

Iskander Hannivoort , Founder of Manukai , Investor, and Airline Captain at Transavia

Natasha Govender-Ropert , Head of AI – Financial Economic Crime at Rabobank

Natasha Govender-Ropert , Head of AI – Financial Economic Crime at Rabobank

Captain van den Broek of the Netherlands Royal Army , and PhD researcher at the University of Twente

Captain van den Broek of the Netherlands Royal Army , and PhD researcher at the University of Twente

--- AI in cybersecurity: “a cat and mouse game” ---

AI is already integral to cybersecurity, from simpler tasks such as filtering spam to anomaly detection and defense automation. But the same technology is also being used to craft more personalized, scalable attacks — especially following the advent of Generative AI.

“We’re seeing a large amount of scam attempts using deepfakes for social engineering purposes,” says Stjepan Picek, Associate Professor of Digital Security at Radboud University. Attackers are also using GenAI tools to automate malware generation, enabling “more diverse attacks with less effort and on a massive scale.”

As to how cybersecurity systems can keep up, Picek says “it’s always been a cat and mouse game.”

“The main difference now is that the cycles are faster,” he adds. A new attack emerges, a defense follows, but another attack soon breaks through.

“With this incremental approach, it’s difficult to say that anything will change fundamentally,” Picek notes. Even if a perfectly secure system existed at the moment, it could still be vulnerable to future threats. “We, as defenders, constantly need to monitor, test, and improve.”

Security of AI and AI for security

Picek and his team at the university are working on two research areas: the security of AI and AI for security. “In the first case, we assess various kinds of machine learning algorithms, try to attack them, and by doing that, we try to propose new defenses that would strengthen them against attacks.”

In the AI for security field, they’re using machine learning and deep learning to perform different types of attacks (such as side-channel attacks), in order to test the security and resilience of various devices like smart or credit cards.

Accuracy trumps safety

One challenge in the cybersecurity space, according to Picek, is that safety by design in AI systems is quite difficult to achieve.

The first reason why is simple. “Machine learning was never designed a priori to be safe and secure. It was designed to be accurate,” he says. The second reason is that safety by design always involves a tradeoff. “Your algorithm will potentially become less accurate. You will spend more time and energy resources to train it. You will need more human expertise to make it.”

As widespread attacks are still relatively limited, he notes, many companies aren’t sufficiently concerned about potential risks. They’re often unwilling to invest in making their systems more secure, especially when this might reduce algorithmic performance.

belief: The organisation assumes that most readers already accept that generative AI significantly amplifies both the scale and sophistication of cyberattacks.
source_quote: more personalized, scalable attacks — especially following the advent of Generative AI
inference_reasoning: The text does not argue for or justify this link but uses it as background explanation, implying a shared understanding with the audience that generative AI naturally leads to more scalable and sophisticated attacks.

belief: AI-based security and risk prediction systems must remain subordinate to and augment human judgment rather than replace it.
source_quote: “That’s why Manukai positions its system as a decision-support tool, not a replacement for human judgment.”
inference_reasoning: By framing the tool explicitly as decision-support and contrasting it with "replacement for human judgment" in the context of liability and black-box concerns, the text presupposes a normative stance that AI should assist, not supplant, human decision-makers in high-stakes security contexts.
source_text:
As AI adoption surges, he warns that security is often an afterthought. “Everyone wants to deploy AI — but safety and security still aren’t high on the list of priorities.”

--- Smarter threat prediction ---

As both digital and physical security challenges grow more complex and intertwined, traditional tools are no longer enough. AI is shifting the paradigm — from reactive monitoring to proactive, real-time threat prediction.

Startups like Manukai are helping drive this shift. The company’s AI-based threat prediction tool — set to go live later this year — aims to alert business travelers about risks ranging from civil unrest to terrorism, before they occur.

“There are certain unique challenges in security at the moment that AI can help address,” Iskander Hannivoort, founder of Manukai, says. He explains that AI enables 24/7 monitoring, faster pattern recognition, and predictive insights — all critical in areas with geopolitical and operational security risks for businesses.

Real-time risk prediction

Manukai’s proprietary system, based on an adapted open-source model, pulls data from sources like news outlets and social media. It analyzes patterns to forecast where and when a threat may occur, using a time- and location-based logic. The goal is full automation, providing users with direct advice — from postponing a trip to navigating to a safe area — without human analysts in the loop.

To be effective, however, the system must be unbiased, Hannivoort explains, though he acknowledges that bias is hard to define. “We need to assess every bit of information before it goes into our prediction model. Is it true and verifiable? What’s the underlying narrative we can detect?”

A second layer of Manukai’s tool is personalization. “Your risk profile is dependent on who you are,” he says. “Our system will know which field you are in and for which groups or countries you or your company’s intellectual property is a high-value target.”

Challenges

A major challenge, Hannivort admits, is understanding how the AI arrives at its predictions, functioning like a black box. “You can only verify the conclusions and try to relatively make sure that they’re free of errors and hallucinations.”

There’s also the question of responsibility. Predictive tools risk both false alarms and missed threats — and with that comes liability. That’s why Manukai positions its system as a decision-support tool, not a replacement for human judgment.

Looking ahead, Hannivoort sees AI-based threat prediction playing a key role in long-term strategic planning — helping companies, governments, and societies identify faster existing and emerging threats.

--- Combating financial crime ---

In the complex realm of financial crime, AI acts as a double-edged sword. Natasha Govender-Ropert from Rabobank, shares how the team leverages AI and analytics to combat threats while navigating critical ethical considerations.

“Banks are highly regulated, which means that there is a significant number of rules and laws we must adhere to, next to having oversight agencies,” she says. “This also influences the type of AI algorithms we implement. We don’t use cutting-edge technology, unless we’re able to explain it.”

AI for anomaly detection

Fraud detection has traditionally relied on rule-based systems, which typically flag suspicious transactions based on certain rules and thresholds. For example, transfers over a certain amount of money over a short period of time.

“The issue with rule-based systems is that they give rise to a large number of false positives, which analysts have to manually review,” she adds. “So our biggest focus area is reducing the amount of false positives.”

belief: In highly regulated domains like banking, explainability and compliance constraints are more important than adopting the most advanced AI technology.
source_quote: “We don’t use cutting-edge technology, unless we’re able to explain it.”
inference_reasoning: By presenting regulation and oversight as shaping algorithm choices and explicitly conditioning the use of cutting-edge AI on explainability, the text implies a strategic priority hierarchy where regulatory compliance and interpretability outweigh the pursuit of state-of-the-art models.
source_text:

belief: Effective fraud detection requires combining human expertise with advanced AI and analytics rather than relying on either alone.
source_quote: This allows human experts to focus on more complex, high-value tasks.
inference_reasoning: By framing AI as handling tedious work so humans can focus on complex tasks, the text presupposes that the optimal approach is a complementary human–AI partnership, not full automation or purely manual work.
source_text:
For this reason, the fraud team is investigating the use of anomaly detection and real-time analytics for fraud detection. “We’re building traditional AI systems in combination with rule-based systems to create a more robust detection algorithm, so we can better understand if our customers’ behavior deviates from normal patterns,” she says.

Compared to rule-based systems with fixed logic, machine learning models operate in multi-dimensional feature spaces, enabling them to learn complex patterns and define more adaptive decision boundaries for fraud detection.

Generative AI: benefits and risks

Govender-Ropert sees GenAI as a valuable tool to improve day-to-day operations. It can significantly reduce manual, tedious work for fraud analysts by assisting with investigations, summarizations, and report writing. This allows human experts to focus on more complex, high-value tasks.

"For instance, we have a chatbot that guides analysts during investigations and retrieves relevant information from multiple databases, providing a clear and concise overview that saves time.”

But the advent of Generative AI has also introduced unprecedented challenges in combating financial crime, Govender-Ropert emphasizes. Fraudsters can now use GenAI to create hyper-realistic fake documents, videos, and even voices. Scams have also become more convincing, with phishing emails and messages that mimic real corporate communications almost perfectly.

Detection of deepfakes remains a developing area. Even experienced professionals may overlook subtle indicators, particularly when not actively assessing content for authenticity.

--- AI in defense: autonomy with human oversight ---

AI is emerging as the next frontier in modern warfare and defence. Earlier this year, State Secretary for Defence Gijs Tuinman announced his intention to invest €310mn in unmanned systems and innovative technologies such as AI.

AI can have several applications within the Dutch armed forces, according to Captain van den Broek of the Royal Netherlands Army. These applications include image recognition, terrain analysis, as well as planning and decision support for units.

Experimenting with autonomous robots

Captain van den Broek also leads the Dutch army’s development of military autonomy and AI. As part of his work in the army as well as his PhD at the University of Twente, he’s currently experimenting with autonomous robots — such as unmanned, remotely controlled vehicles. Such robots can be used for tasks including reconnaissance missions and cleaning high-danger zones like landmines and radioactive areas.

Controlled autonomy

The level of autonomy is entirely use-case dependent, Captain van den Broek says. “Is it just navigation or are we talking about swarming missions with multiple vehicles, or even drones all working together?”

He emphasizes that robots and AI can assist with data and a variety of tasks, but the use of lethal force must always remain a human decision. This principle guides the development of autonomous systems in the Dutch armed forces, where there is no place for self-learning weapons that decide in real-time who or what to target.

All model development and training must be completed and verified in advance. “During execution, you need a fully trained and validated model that has passed all necessary checks,” Captain van den Broek explains. This ensures what he calls "controlled autonomy:" systems that are capable but predictable — and always under human command.

Trust through transparency

Trust is central. “If we want to use autonomous robots,” he says, “commanders must trust that they will behave as expected.” Part of Captain van den Broek’s work involves not only refining the technology, but also showing unit commanders exactly how these systems work and what decisions they’re making in practice.

“These systems should support, not replace human decision-making,” he emphasizes.

--- What does “robust” AI really mean? ---

belief: Ethical and responsible AI practices are becoming a de facto expectation and baseline requirement for serious AI development efforts.
source_quote: AI systems can reinforce bias, invade privacy, or make unsafe decisions,  unless ethics are embedded from day one.
inference_reasoning: By framing ethics as something that must be embedded "from day one" to avoid serious harms, the text presupposes that any credible AI development is now expected to incorporate ethical and responsible practices as a standard, not an optional add-on.
source_text:
According to the experts we spoke with, the essence of AI robustness depends on the field and the use case for which a model is deployed.

From a cybersecurity perspective, model robustness ideally means resilience against attacks, Picek says. “Once you train your model in the presence of a specific attack, you make it more robust against similar attacks that might happen,” he explains. Achieving this kind of robustness depends not only on the model architecture and threat type, but also on who controls the training process — whether it’s handled internally or outsourced.

“In military language, AI or autonomy robustness means that the systems will always work across different environments, scenarios, and threats,” Captain van den Broek says. This includes high performance in various terrains, weather conditions, and different types of tasks such as traditional warfare or peacekeeping missions.

“Robustness starts with data quality and relevance," says Govender-Ropert. It’s also about checking a model’s thresholds, verifying its behavior through testing, and actively monitoring it after deployment — especially since models can drift over time as new data enters the system.

--- Building ethical and responsible AI ---

AI systems can reinforce bias, invade privacy, or make unsafe decisions,  unless ethics are embedded from day one.

Responsible AI in practice

For Govender-Ropert, ethical AI refers to the broader societal and philosophical question of what is good or harmful, while responsible AI is about putting those values into practice. At Rabobank, this means building systems that are transparent, explainable, secure, and fair. These principles are embedded into the bank’s model development lifecycle.

But fairness isn't a universal standard and different organizations may interpret it differently, Govender-Ropert emphasizes. What matters is that teams make conscious decisions about what fairness means in their context and translate them into clear guidelines for development and testing.

“We need to understand that the decisions we make, how we build these models, and how we use them can impact people’s lives.”

Ethics start with intent, not compliance

Stjepan Picek believes that ethical and safe AI begins with intent, not just compliance with regulations such as the EU’s AI Act. “We, as humans, developers, owners, and users of models, need to acknowledge that AI security is an important goal,” he says.

Fairness is a critical element for Picek as well. “At the moment, we see more negative consequences from biased algorithms than from insecure algorithms.”

Guardrails without stifling innovation

Iskander Hannivoort agrees that safety and fairness are essential — but warns that over-regulation may hamper Europe’s competitiveness in the AI race. “We want to innovate, but we also need guardrails,” he says. “That balance is hard.”

Keeping humans in the loop

In the defense sector, Captain van den Broek stresses that responsible AI means incorporating human oversight at every level: from commanders who must trust the systems they deploy, to political and strategic leaders who decide when and how these technologies are used, and ultimately to the society they serve.

“Transparency, explainability, and human oversight: that’s what responsible autonomy looks like,” he says.

Ethical deployment, he argues, is about protecting people. Yet in our imperfect world, these same technologies can also be weaponized in violation of human rights. “This is a serious ethical debate,” Captain van den Broek says. “But not developing these technologies could leave us at a disadvantage against enemies who do.”

--- The Netherlands: positioned to lead or lagging behind? ---

The Netherlands has made visible progress in embedding ethical governance into AI development, especially in the banking sector, says Govender-Ropert, speaking from her personal experience in ING and Rabobank.

belief: Maintaining international competitiveness in AI requires balancing regulatory guardrails with sufficient freedom to innovate, rather than prioritizing one at the expense of the other.
