belief: Reliable chatbot evaluation requires combining multiple complementary metrics rather than relying on a single method.
source_quote: In practice, LLM and non-LLM based metrics were used together to get a holistic picture of the evaluation.
inference_reasoning: Describing the combination of LLM and non-LLM metrics as giving a "holistic picture" presupposes that no single metric type is sufficient and that a multi-metric approach is the appropriate strategy for reliability.
source_text:
Non-Advice : The LLM judges if the answer stays clear from giving specific financial advice but instead informs and educates the user about their options. This is critical for risk mitigation and legal compliance.

Tone : Checking that the chatbot response matches the tone, style, and voice of the user. This evaluates how well the chatbot handles aspects like formality, empathy, and directness to give a consistent and positive User Experience.

Tone : Checking that the chatbot response matches the tone, style, and voice of the user. This evaluates how well the chatbot handles aspects like formality, empathy, and directness to give a consistent and positive User Experience.

There are limitations with LLM judges, however. They suffer from the same shortcoming as the chatbot itself: the variability in their responses and bias. They can occasionally produce outright wrong evaluations or misunderstand the user intent, or the intent of the expected response. Additionally, they can be relatively slow (and expensive) to run: having 10 judges, requires 10 requests to an LLM judge to evaluate a single chatbot answer. This directly impacts the speed of iteration and development feedback, which can be crucial in a two-week hackathon.

Therefore, the team came up with non-LLM based metrics that are fast to run, reproducible, and deterministic. For example:

URL checker: Checks that the links in a reply do actually exist in the retrieval database.

URL checker: Checks that the links in a reply do actually exist in the retrieval database.

Readability: Uses the Flesch Reading Ease score to determine how readable a chatbot reply is. We aim for a score of 70, which corresponds to 7th grade reading level.

Readability: Uses the Flesch Reading Ease score to determine how readable a chatbot reply is. We aim for a score of 70, which corresponds to 7th grade reading level.

Word count: Simply counting the words in the chatbot reply to see if the bot keeps it concise.

Word count: Simply counting the words in the chatbot reply to see if the bot keeps it concise.

In practice, LLM and non-LLM based metrics were used together to get a holistic picture of the evaluation.

At this point, it is nevertheless worth mentioning that evaluations alone are not sufficient to create a reliable chatbot system. LLMs can generate plausible answers even when their input is corrupted due to a bug in the code, as in one instance when the chatbot started greeting users again mid-conversation. Such issues can remain silent unless they are explicitly tested for, making classical software best practices, such as unit testing and monitoring, more important than ever.

Throughout the challenge, the evaluation team monitored the work of the other teams to ensure that the whole group made progress. New features often introduced new issues, which in turn led to the addition of new test cases and judges to monitor those problems and implement fixes.

As a result, the actual evaluation scores kept fluctuating significantly during the challenge. This was because the evaluation framework itself was not fixed but constantly changed together with the capabilities of the chatbot, becoming progressively stricter as more test cases and judges were added.

Consequently, although we made a lot of qualitative improvements to the chatbot, the evaluation scores over the two weeks do not directly reflect that. Nevertheless, the work of the evaluation team was invaluable as it provided guidance and feedback to the other teams.

--- Work package 2: Understanding and Informing ---

The “Understanding and Informing” team was key in customizing the chatbot. The first step to tailoring a generic chatbot to a specific context is to write a system prompt that instructs the chatbot about its role, its tasks, boundaries, and capabilities.

The core objective of this work package was to:

--- Work package 2: Understanding and Informing ---

The “Understanding and Informing” team was key in customizing the chatbot. The first step to tailoring a generic chatbot to a specific context is to write a system prompt that instructs the chatbot about its role, its tasks, boundaries, and capabilities.

The core objective of this work package was to:

belief: Minimizing user cognitive load during conversations is essential for effectively supporting users in financial distress.
source_quote: ask only one focused question at a time to avoid overwhelming the user
inference_reasoning: Framing multiple questions as potentially overwhelming and explicitly designing a "One Question" rule implies a deeper value that reducing cognitive burden is crucial when interacting with distressed users.
source_text:
Understand: Establish a deep understanding of the user's financial context, especially when they are in severe financial distress. Obtain this information purely through empathetic conversation.

Understand: Establish a deep understanding of the user's financial context, especially when they are in severe financial distress. Obtain this information purely through empathetic conversation.

Inform: Educate them with relevant and accurate financial knowledge.

Inform: Educate them with relevant and accurate financial knowledge.

Personalize: Adapt the chatbot's tone and communication style (formal vs. informal, empathetic vs. factual) to the user’s style of communication while preserving their anonymity.

Personalize: Adapt the chatbot's tone and communication style (formal vs. informal, empathetic vs. factual) to the user’s style of communication while preserving their anonymity.

In collaboration with ING’s conversation specialist and the evaluation team, this team formulated explicit prompts to instruct the bot to adapt its answer tone based on the detected user persona. The team adopted an investigation strategy to ask only one focused question at a time to avoid overwhelming the user and to gather the minimum necessary information for a hand-off or a tailored response (the "One Question" rule).

Prompting – even begging – the chatbot LLM to only ask one question at a time didn’t convince the chatbot to do so. Very often it would ask multiple questions. To address this, the team found a simple trick: instead of letting the chatbot generate free text, they enforced a structured output that allowed only the following three components:

Reasoning: The LLM generates a step-by-step approach to analyze the situation and give an answer. This is not shown to the user.

Reasoning: The LLM generates a step-by-step approach to analyze the situation and give an answer. This is not shown to the user.

Response: The main answer containing the requested information.

Response: The main answer containing the requested information.

Follow-up question: The follow-up question to ask.

Follow-up question: The follow-up question to ask.

By enforcing this structure, the chatbot consistently asked only one follow-up question. Additionally, the response quality also improved because the LLM had room to create a plan and reflect on its instructions in the reasoning stage, before creating a response. This technique is known as “chain-of-thought”. Current LLMs perform this reasoning (also called “thinking”) automatically and don’t have to be prompted to do so. However, that process can not be controlled, which is why we made it explicit in the chatbot output structure.

>>> Multi-agent Architecture <<<

Writing a single prompt for all the different scenarios and edge cases can lead to an overly large system prompt, which may contain conflicting instructions that can confuse the chabot. Therefore, the team set up a multi-agent architecture in collaboration with the “Tool Use” team. In this architecture, each user question is forwarded to a chatbot “within” the chatbot (a so-called “subagent”). Each subagent is responsible for a specific topic, such as mortgages, investments, or credit cards. These subagents have their own prompts and also their own specific tools (see Tool Use ).

In addition to the topic-specific subagents, the team added a “customer intake” subagent, responsible for gathering information about the user’s situation. This agent is the default agent being activated if the topic or the intent of the question is not yet clear.

But how do we decide which subagent to invoke for a certain user question? We can use another agent! For every user question, the conversation is passed to a faster language model that has only one task: return the name of the subagent that should process this question. We call this the “routing agent.”

belief: Decomposing complex tasks across specialized agents is a more reliable way to control large language model behavior than relying on a single, comprehensive prompt.
source_quote: Writing a single prompt for all the different scenarios and edge cases can lead to an overly large system prompt, which may contain conflicting instructions that can confuse the chabot. Therefore, the team set up a multi-agent architecture
inference_reasoning: The contrast between an "overly large" confusing prompt and the chosen multi-agent setup implies an underlying strategic belief that specialization and modularization yield more predictable and controllable LLM behavior.
source_text:
Understand: Establish a deep understanding of the user's financial context, especially when they are in severe financial distress. Obtain this information purely through empathetic conversation.

Understand: Establish a deep understanding of the user's financial context, especially when they are in severe financial distress. Obtain this information purely through empathetic conversation.

Inform: Educate them with relevant and accurate financial knowledge.

Inform: Educate them with relevant and accurate financial knowledge.

Personalize: Adapt the chatbot's tone and communication style (formal vs. informal, empathetic vs. factual) to the user’s style of communication while preserving their anonymity.

Personalize: Adapt the chatbot's tone and communication style (formal vs. informal, empathetic vs. factual) to the user’s style of communication while preserving their anonymity.

In collaboration with ING’s conversation specialist and the evaluation team, this team formulated explicit prompts to instruct the bot to adapt its answer tone based on the detected user persona. The team adopted an investigation strategy to ask only one focused question at a time to avoid overwhelming the user and to gather the minimum necessary information for a hand-off or a tailored response (the "One Question" rule).

Prompting – even begging – the chatbot LLM to only ask one question at a time didn’t convince the chatbot to do so. Very often it would ask multiple questions. To address this, the team found a simple trick: instead of letting the chatbot generate free text, they enforced a structured output that allowed only the following three components:

Reasoning: The LLM generates a step-by-step approach to analyze the situation and give an answer. This is not shown to the user.

Reasoning: The LLM generates a step-by-step approach to analyze the situation and give an answer. This is not shown to the user.

Response: The main answer containing the requested information.

Response: The main answer containing the requested information.

Follow-up question: The follow-up question to ask.

Follow-up question: The follow-up question to ask.

By enforcing this structure, the chatbot consistently asked only one follow-up question. Additionally, the response quality also improved because the LLM had room to create a plan and reflect on its instructions in the reasoning stage, before creating a response. This technique is known as “chain-of-thought”. Current LLMs perform this reasoning (also called “thinking”) automatically and don’t have to be prompted to do so. However, that process can not be controlled, which is why we made it explicit in the chatbot output structure.

>>> Multi-agent Architecture <<<

Writing a single prompt for all the different scenarios and edge cases can lead to an overly large system prompt, which may contain conflicting instructions that can confuse the chabot. Therefore, the team set up a multi-agent architecture in collaboration with the “Tool Use” team. In this architecture, each user question is forwarded to a chatbot “within” the chatbot (a so-called “subagent”). Each subagent is responsible for a specific topic, such as mortgages, investments, or credit cards. These subagents have their own prompts and also their own specific tools (see Tool Use ).

In addition to the topic-specific subagents, the team added a “customer intake” subagent, responsible for gathering information about the user’s situation. This agent is the default agent being activated if the topic or the intent of the question is not yet clear.

But how do we decide which subagent to invoke for a certain user question? We can use another agent! For every user question, the conversation is passed to a faster language model that has only one task: return the name of the subagent that should process this question. We call this the “routing agent.”

belief: Chatbot responses in this domain are only trustworthy if they are grounded in pre-approved, high-quality external sources rather than generated purely from the language model.
source_quote: because every informational response needed to be grounded in trusted sources
inference_reasoning: By framing ungrounded responses as a problem and mandating retrieval for every interaction, the text presupposes that source-grounding is a necessary condition for trustworthiness, not just a design choice.
source_text:
One special type of agent in our architecture is always active: the RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) agent. This agent generates search queries  for the retrieval engine to fetch websites that are relevant to the user question. Every chatbot answer should be grounded in a website, which is why the RAG agent is constantly active – while for simple inputs like “Hello!,” it  can also decide not to perform a search.

Introducing multiple subagents created a new challenge. Each subagent required instructions for answer style, which led to duplicated prompt content and, over time, inconsistent responses across subagents. To resolve this, the team added a “final answer agent.” This agent synthesizes the information collected by the subagents as well as website snippets from the RAG agent. It then formulates a concise, understandable response that is sent back to the user. The prompt for the final answer agent includes instructions for the tone, style, and structure of the response.

--- Work package 3: Tool use & Retrieval ---

Tools are pieces of software that the chatbot can use to perform a certain task, such as calculations, information retrieval, or even scheduling appointments. For each user input, the chatbot can decide whether it can answer directly or whether a tool is required. If a tool is needed, the chatbot generates the input for that tool. The tool is then executed and the results are fed back to the chatbot. Based on these results, the chatbot can either generate a response for the user or decide to invoke another tool, repeating this process as necessary.

Each of the six agents has access to a specific tool set which it needs to perform its duties. In total, the team implemented 22 tools during the challenge.

For a financial application for example, calculators are an important tool as language models are not reliable with math. Current LLM models can do simple calculations like additions and multiplications but struggle with exponentials like compound interest calculations for investment returns.

>>> Retrieval <<<

Retrieval refers to the process of collecting approved websites that are relevant to the user question and can provide this information to the chatbot, so it can generate a response with verifiable sources

Subsequently, the websites are fed to the chatbot so that it can generate a response with sources that the user can check.

Initially, retrieval was implemented as a tool that individual agents could invoke when needed. However, because every informational response needed to be grounded in trusted sources, we didn’t want to rely on the chatbot or the subagents to decide whether they would use the tool. This is why we added a dedicated retrieval subagent (the “RAG agent” mentioned above) which is invoked for every user interaction and generates a retrieval query for the retrieval engine.

For the retrieval engine we had several options at the start of the challenge:

A self-hosted vector database that is integrated with the chatbot application

A self-hosted vector database that is integrated with the chatbot application

A vector database hosted by Google Cloud (Vertex RAG)

A vector database hosted by Google Cloud (Vertex RAG)

A hybrid text search and vector search database, also hosted on Google Cloud (Vertex AI Search)

A hybrid text search and vector search database, also hosted on Google Cloud (Vertex AI Search)

The retrieval corpus consisted of a collection of websites from ING.nl , Geldfit.nl , Wijzeringeldzaken.nl , NIBUD.nl that we downloaded, cleaned from cookie banners and irrelevant layout elements, and saved as text files.

To determine which retrieval engine performs, the team created an evaluation pipeline that used synthetic question-answer pairs, generated from the retrieval corpus with the following methodology:

belief: Generative chatbots in financial services are inherently risky and will behave unpredictably unless constrained by strong safety mechanisms.
source_quote: Guardrails, defined as rigid safety mechanisms, are necessary to contain the chatbot's unpredictable generative capacity
inference_reasoning: By asserting that rigid guardrails are "necessary" to "contain" unpredictability, the text presupposes that such chatbots are intrinsically prone to unsafe or uncontrolled behavior in this domain unless tightly constrained.
source_text:
Source Document Sampling: We selected a diverse and representative set of websites from the knowledge base. For each selection, we identified a few paragraphs or "chunks of text" that are thematically or contextually related. This ensures that the generated questions require a multi-website or multi-chunk synthesis to answer.

Question and Answer Generation: We employed an LLM to analyze the selected, related chunks of text. The LLM's task was to:

Generate a Question: Create a complex, natural language question that requires synthesizing information from the selected chunks to form a complete answer. These questions should simulate genuine user queries.

Mark the Source: The original chunks of text that contain the necessary information to answer the generated question must be explicitly marked and associated with that question-answer pair. These marked chunks serve as the "ground truth" source for validation during the retrieval phase.

Scaling the Process: This process must be repeated a substantial number of    times to create a diverse evaluation dataset. The goal is to cover various document topics, question types (e.g., factual, comparative, descriptive), and levels of difficulty.

Retrieval Metrics:

Recall: This metric measures the retrieval system's ability to retrieve all relevant pieces of information necessary to form a correct and complete answer.

Recall: This metric measures the retrieval system's ability to retrieve all relevant pieces of information necessary to form a correct and complete answer.

Precision: This metric measures the efficiency and accuracy of the retrieval system, focusing on minimizing noise and irrelevant context.

Precision: This metric measures the efficiency and accuracy of the retrieval system, focusing on minimizing noise and irrelevant context.

Together with the evaluation system, the agent team concluded that Vertex AI search provided the best results. This is not surprising, as it is based on the very same technology that Google Search is based on as well.

--- Work package 4: Guardrails ---

Developing a chatbot for a financial institution presents unique challenges because the topic is both personal and sensitive. It should provide helpful information to vulnerable customers without crossing ethical, regulatory, or operational boundaries.

Guardrails, defined as rigid safety mechanisms, are necessary to contain the chatbot's unpredictable generative capacity and ensure it remains helpful without becoming illegal, unethical, or starting to hallucinate.

The primary objective was to secure the chatbot against six specific risks:

Regulatory Risk (Financial Advice)

Regulatory Risk (Financial Advice)

Guarantee the chatbot informs and educates but never advises. The objective is to detect and block responses that contain specific financial recommendations.

Privacy Risk (PII)

Privacy Risk (PII)

Safeguard customer data in line with GDPR and ING guidelines. The objective is to identify and redact Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from user inputs (e.g., names, addresses, IBANs) to prevent sensitive data from being processed or stored.

Ethical Risk (Bias)

Ethical Risk (Bias)

Prevent harmful stereotypes and bias in chatbot responses. The objective is to monitor and filter output to ensure the chatbot uses neutral, inclusive language.

Brand Risk (Competitors & Ads)

Brand Risk (Competitors & Ads)

Protect ING's reputation and commercial interests. The objective is to prevent the chatbot from mentioning competitor banks or advertising specific commercial third parties.

Reliability Risk (Link Hallucinations)

Reliability Risk (Link Hallucinations)

Eliminate "link hallucinations" to maintain user trust. The objective is to verify that every URL provided by the chatbot actually exists and is accessible.

Scope Risk (On-Topic)

Scope Risk (On-Topic)

belief: Protecting users and the institution from regulatory, privacy, ethical, brand, reliability, and scope risks is more important than maximizing the chatbot’s unconstrained capabilities.
source_quote: The primary objective was to secure the chatbot against six specific risks
inference_reasoning: Framing security against listed risks as the "primary objective" implies a value hierarchy where risk mitigation and protection take precedence over other potential goals like breadth of functionality or expressiveness.

belief: Protecting users from financial harm and privacy risks is more important than maximizing the chatbot’s freedom to give personalized financial advice.
source_quote: we could push the envelope further and relax some of the strict requirements like the PII filtering.
inference_reasoning: Describing the relaxation of strict PII and advice constraints as “pushing the envelope” and a special case for a final demonstration presupposes that the default, preferred state is strong protection of users’ financial safety and privacy, even at the cost of limiting advice quality or personalization.
source_text:
Restrict the chatbot on financial health use cases. The objective is to detect off-topic queries and steer the conversation back to the core topic.

Simple keyword filtering was deemed insufficient for creating robust guardrails, as it lacks the understanding required to detect nuance, or context. Instead, the team implemented an LLM-as-a-Judge framework , very similar to the LLM judges in the evaluation team.

There are two types of guardrails:

The input guardrails that guard our application against malicious or unwanted user inputs. Examples are questions about unrelated topics or even attempts to “jail-break” the chatbot, where a malicious user tries to coerce the chatbot into undesired behavior.

The input guardrails that guard our application against malicious or unwanted user inputs. Examples are questions about unrelated topics or even attempts to “jail-break” the chatbot, where a malicious user tries to coerce the chatbot into undesired behavior.

The output guardrails that check the output of the chatbot, in this case specifically the final answer agent. This is where we check for unwanted financial advice or biases in the responses.

The output guardrails that check the output of the chatbot, in this case specifically the final answer agent. This is where we check for unwanted financial advice or biases in the responses.

For each guardrail, smaller, specialized language models with a singular clear purpose act as a guarding layer . These "Judge" agents evaluate the user’s input and the chatbot’s draft output against the pre-defined safety concerns. The process is broken down into three steps:

The Draft (Hidden): The message (user question or chatbot answer) is intercepted and temporarily held.

The Draft (Hidden): The message (user question or chatbot answer) is intercepted and temporarily held.

The Trial (The Judge): A separate, specifically instructed AI evaluates the text against the guardrails.

The Trial (The Judge): A separate, specifically instructed AI evaluates the text against the guardrails.

The Verdict (Shown): The guardrail judge issues a verdict. Safe: The message is released. Unsafe: The message is redacted, refused, rephrased, or adjusted before being sent.

The Verdict (Shown): The guardrail judge issues a verdict.

Safe: The message is released.

Safe: The message is released.

Unsafe: The message is redacted, refused, rephrased, or adjusted before being sent.

Unsafe: The message is redacted, refused, rephrased, or adjusted before being sent.

Let’s look a this process for the PII handling: The system detects specific entities (Names, IBANs, Addresses) and replaces them with placeholders ([REDACTED_NAME]) or generalizes them (Age 50 → [range_age: 50-60]) before the conversation is processed by the main chatbot model.

You may ask yourself now: How can I get useful, customized answers if the chatbot is not allowed to give financial advice or can see any personal information?

This was indeed an ongoing dilemma during the challenge, and we concluded that for the sake of the final demonstration, we could push the envelope further and relax some of the strict requirements like the PII filtering.

--- Conclusion ---

Over the course of two weeks, 14 participants from across KickstartAI's partner companies turned a basic chatbot prototype into a financial health assistant. The resulting system could address the challenge of making financial support more accessible by lowering the barrier to entry through an anonymous, empathetic AI interface.

belief: Anonymous, empathetic AI interfaces are an effective way to lower psychological and practical barriers that prevent people from seeking financial support.
source_quote: lowering the barrier to entry through an anonymous, empathetic AI interface
inference_reasoning: Framing anonymity and empathy as the mechanism for “lowering the barrier to entry” presupposes that such interfaces successfully address existing reluctance or obstacles to accessing financial help, implying a belief in their effectiveness for this purpose.

belief: Responsible deployment of generative AI in sensitive domains is primarily a socio-technical design problem rather than a purely model-prompting problem.
source_quote: building reliable AI systems requires more than prompt engineering alone
inference_reasoning: By contrasting "prompt engineering" with the broader set of practices (architecture, evaluation, safety layers), the text presupposes that reliability and responsibility hinge on system and process design, not just on how the model is prompted.
source_text:
The technical innovations developed during the challenge – the multi-agent architecture, the evaluation framework combining LLM and non-LLM judges, the retrieval system, and the guardrail approach – represent more than just engineering achievements. They demonstrate how generative AI can be responsibly deployed in sensitive domains like personal finance. By separating concerns across specialized agents, enforcing structured outputs, and implementing multiple layers of safety checks, the team created a system that balances empathy and personalization with regulatory compliance and user privacy.

Perhaps most importantly, the challenge revealed that building reliable AI systems requires more than prompt engineering alone. The evaluation-driven development approach, the synthetic data generation pipeline, and the classical software testing practices proved essential for iterating quickly and catching issues that LLMs might otherwise silently mask. While tensions remain – between personalization and privacy, between usefulness and compliance – the foundation is in place for a chatbot that could meaningfully support the 47% of Dutch households facing financial vulnerability, while also helping everyone stay informed about their financial health, both now and in the future.

--- Lessons Learned ---

One agent, one job : Multi-agent architectures with specialized subagents outperform monolithic prompts, reducing conflicting instructions and improving response quality.

One agent, one job : Multi-agent architectures with specialized subagents outperform monolithic prompts, reducing conflicting instructions and improving response quality.

Structure beats begging : Enforce structured outputs (JSON) rather than prompting the LLM to follow rules – it's the difference between "please ask one question" and making it impossible to ask more.

Structure beats begging : Enforce structured outputs (JSON) rather than prompting the LLM to follow rules – it's the difference between "please ask one question" and making it impossible to ask more.

LLMs hide bugs : Generative AI can produce plausible responses even when code is broken, making classical testing (unit tests, monitoring) more critical than ever.

LLMs hide bugs : Generative AI can produce plausible responses even when code is broken, making classical testing (unit tests, monitoring) more critical than ever.

Synthetic data needs human touch : Generated evaluation datasets are scalable but require human review and correction to be realistic and useful

Synthetic data needs human touch : Generated evaluation datasets are scalable but require human review and correction to be realistic and useful

Fast feedback loops win : Combine quick non-LLM metrics (word count, readability scores, URL validation) with slower LLM judges to maintain development velocity.

Fast feedback loops win : Combine quick non-LLM metrics (word count, readability scores, URL validation) with slower LLM judges to maintain development velocity.

Let them think : Explicit chain-of-thought reasoning in agent outputs improves instruction-following and response quality – make the hidden reasoning visible and structured.

Let them think : Explicit chain-of-thought reasoning in agent outputs improves instruction-following and response quality – make the hidden reasoning visible and structured.

Evaluation drives everything : Building the evaluation framework alongside the chatbot, not after, enables rapid iteration and prevents regression.

Evaluation drives everything : Building the evaluation framework alongside the chatbot, not after, enables rapid iteration and prevents regression.

The personalization paradox : There's an inherent tension between creating helpful, empathetic, personalized experiences and maintaining privacy, professionalism, and regulatory compliance – acknowledge and navigate it explicitly.

belief: Evaluation and testing should be treated as first-class, continuous components of AI system development rather than as afterthoughts.
source_quote: Building the evaluation framework alongside the chatbot, not after
inference_reasoning: The contrast between "alongside" and "after" presupposes that evaluation is normally postponed but ought to be integrated from the start and maintained throughout development as a core practice.

=== Employee Spotlight: Jorrit van der Laan ===

Meet Jorrit van der Laan, Machine Learning Engineer at KickstartAI. With a background in mathematics and data science, he brings an analytical mindset and a passion for solving complex, real-world problems through AI.

Let’s dive into his journey into the field, what drives his work, and how he aims to create meaningful impact through technology.

>>> What’s your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I’m a Machine Learning Engineer, working with our team to build AI applications for different companies. At the moment, I’m involved in two partner projects: one with ING and one with bol.com.

>>> Can you share your journey into AI? <<<

I did a bachelor’s in mathematics and only started coding during my master’s in data science, which I chose because I wanted to move towards technical applications. Since then, I’ve been working as a machine learning and data scientist in both startups and consulting companies.

​​What’s something in our projects that genuinely excites you?

I find it very exciting understanding companies from the inside and working on completely new problems in different industries. What I like about our project with ING, for instance, is that we’re working on a chatbot for financial health assistance, and we’re developing it completely from scratch.

>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

One part of me enjoys solving puzzles and finding patterns — and that’s what you do both in mathematics and machine learning. But another part of me really likes interacting with people from different backgrounds. And that’s what’s special about the AI field: you can be deeply technical and also collaborate with all kinds of people, understand their perspectives, and see how your work can have an actual impact.

Focusing on both social and business impact is also what I really enjoy about working at KickstartAI.

>>> What are your go-to AI tools? <<<

I regularly use coding assistants, such as Codex, although I don’t have a strong preference for a specific provider, to be honest. I also use chat-based tools like ChatGPT. I really like how useful they are for learning.

For example, I was recently working with a python package I hadn’t used before. I could just ask, “Can you explain the overall idea and why it was introduced?” and then follow up with questions like, “I don’t understand this, can you explain a bit more?” It’s a great method for that kind of step-by-step learning. You can keep asking questions until you fully understand.

>>> What’s your advice to people considering a career in AI? <<<

My advice would be: don’t get too attached to specific tools or ML techniques.

The AI field is changing so quickly that a specific technique or framework you learn today might already be outdated five years from now. For example, locally training a language model used to be common, but with all the advanced pre-trained models available now, it’s not needed anymore. Although fine-tuning still happens, of course.

Looking back, I’m glad I studied mathematics. Math teaches you to think analytically: to break problems down into smaller pieces, to make assumptions, and reason your way to conclusions. These are skills you’ll always need.

belief: AI work is expected to balance technical excellence with meaningful social and business impact.
source_quote: Focusing on both social and business impact is also what I really enjoy about working at KickstartAI.
inference_reasoning: Describing the dual focus on social and business impact as a distinctive positive feature of working at the organisation implies an underlying expectation that valuable AI work should integrate both dimensions, not just technical performance.
source_text:
The personalization paradox : There's an inherent tension between creating helpful, empathetic, personalized experiences and maintaining privacy, professionalism, and regulatory compliance – acknowledge and navigate it explicitly.

=== Why (and how) we need to measure data diversity in language models ===

Recent advances in language models are typically credited to improvements of architecture and ever-larger datasets. But a parallel conversation has been building – in research labs and across industry – around a more fundamental question: not just how much data these models are trained on, but what that data actually contains.

Within this context, Dong Nguyen – Associate Professor of Computer Science at Utrecht University – points out that data diversity is frequently cited as an important factor, and as one of the main reasons why models may show bias or fail on unusual cases during interaction.

“But when you take a step back,’ Nguyen says, “it’s very unclear what we mean with data diversity.”

The term, she notes, can refer to many different things, and without the necessary theoretical and practical insights, it’s difficult to make substantial claims about data diversity’s role. “How much does diversity matter? Which types of diversity are relevant? At what stages of training does diversity matter the most, and does its importance vary across tasks?”

These open questions are at the core of DataDivers , the research project Nguyen is currently leading. Funding by the EU and part of the NLP and Society Lab of Utrecht University, the project aims to examine how data diversity can be defined and measured in datasets, how it relates to the behaviour of models, and how it might be taken into account to develop fairer and more robust models.

“We’re focusing on LLMs at the moment, but a lot of the things that we do are quite general,” Nguyen notes, adding that the methods are intended to apply more broadly across Natural Language Processing (NLP) beyond large language models alone.

--- Measuring data diversity ---

According to Nguyen, the team’s main goal is to develop different methods that make it possible to measure data diversity more systematically. At the moment, their work focuses on two broad axes. The first is what she describes as stylistic diversity.

“Within any language,” she explains, “people don’t always write or speak in the same way. Expression varies depending on background, but also on the situation: writing a job application, for instance, is very different from speaking with a friend. The question then becomes how this kind of variation is represented in datasets, and whether it can be measured in a meaningful way.”

The second axis is semantic diversity, which relates more directly to the meaning of text. This includes variation in topics and subject matter, and how such differences are distributed across a dataset. Although the project will later look at other types of diversity, these two were chosen first because they’re expected to be relevant across many different applications.

belief: The organisation assumes that current discourse about data diversity in language models is conceptually under-specified and therefore insufficient for guiding reliable claims or practices.
source_quote: it’s very unclear what we mean with data diversity
inference_reasoning: Framing the meaning of data diversity as “very unclear” in the context of widespread citation implies a critical stance that existing uses of the term lack rigor and are inadequate for sound research and industry decisions.
source_text:
>>> What part of the AI field interests/inspires you the most? <<<

I really like both ends of the spectrum. On one hand, I’m interested in the theoretical side: using AI for fundamental research, such as supporting mathematical proofs. On the other hand, I’m drawn to applied AI, like predicting patient inflow in hospitals, or developing AI tools for wound detection. I think healthcare is a particularly interesting field.

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

Did you know that Beethoven wrote 32 piano sonatas? Well, if you show me a random page of any of them, I can tell you which sonata it is and which section of the piece the page comes from.

=== Why (and how) we need to measure data diversity in language models ===

Recent advances in language models are typically credited to improvements of architecture and ever-larger datasets. But a parallel conversation has been building – in research labs and across industry – around a more fundamental question: not just how much data these models are trained on, but what that data actually contains.

Within this context, Dong Nguyen – Associate Professor of Computer Science at Utrecht University – points out that data diversity is frequently cited as an important factor, and as one of the main reasons why models may show bias or fail on unusual cases during interaction.

“But when you take a step back,’ Nguyen says, “it’s very unclear what we mean with data diversity.”

The term, she notes, can refer to many different things, and without the necessary theoretical and practical insights, it’s difficult to make substantial claims about data diversity’s role. “How much does diversity matter? Which types of diversity are relevant? At what stages of training does diversity matter the most, and does its importance vary across tasks?”

These open questions are at the core of DataDivers , the research project Nguyen is currently leading. Funding by the EU and part of the NLP and Society Lab of Utrecht University, the project aims to examine how data diversity can be defined and measured in datasets, how it relates to the behaviour of models, and how it might be taken into account to develop fairer and more robust models.

“We’re focusing on LLMs at the moment, but a lot of the things that we do are quite general,” Nguyen notes, adding that the methods are intended to apply more broadly across Natural Language Processing (NLP) beyond large language models alone.

--- Measuring data diversity ---

According to Nguyen, the team’s main goal is to develop different methods that make it possible to measure data diversity more systematically. At the moment, their work focuses on two broad axes. The first is what she describes as stylistic diversity.

“Within any language,” she explains, “people don’t always write or speak in the same way. Expression varies depending on background, but also on the situation: writing a job application, for instance, is very different from speaking with a friend. The question then becomes how this kind of variation is represented in datasets, and whether it can be measured in a meaningful way.”

The second axis is semantic diversity, which relates more directly to the meaning of text. This includes variation in topics and subject matter, and how such differences are distributed across a dataset. Although the project will later look at other types of diversity, these two were chosen first because they’re expected to be relevant across many different applications.

belief: Improving the measurement of data diversity is a necessary precondition for moving dataset design from ad hoc heuristics toward a more systematic, science-like practice.
source_quote: it is difficult to investigate these questions systematically without concrete frameworks or tools to measure diversity in the first place
inference_reasoning: By framing the lack of concrete diversity metrics as what makes systematic investigation difficult, the text implies that better measurement is a prerequisite for replacing heuristic, ad hoc dataset decisions with a more rigorous approach.
source_text:
From a technical perspective, Nguyen explains that the approaches to measuring stylistic and semantic diversity are quite similar. The core idea in both cases is to map texts into a vector space. For semantic diversity, texts that are similar in meaning end up close to each other in that space. For stylistic diversity, the goal is comparable: texts written in similar styles are placed near each other, while texts written in very different styles are positioned further apart.

“In this way, a dataset can be represented as a large cloud of points in a space,” Nguyen says. From there, it’s possible to apply different metrics to quantify diversity.

“We’re currently trying to categorize the different ways of measuring diversity and look at the pros and cons of each metric. At the same time, we’re setting up more systematic experiments to investigate which metrics work better than others and to formalize them further.”

--- Potential impact on models ---

Once clearer metrics for data diversity are in place, they can be used to investigate more systematically the impact of data composition on model behavior, Nguyen explains.

“If we vary the diversity in the dataset along different  axes,” she says, “it becomes possible to study whether this influences how well the models perform on certain tasks or the kind of linguistic variation present in their outputs.”

Building on this, Nguyen ties data diversity more broadly to the learning process of language models. She compares it to human learning: when acquiring a new language or skill, it would be limiting to rely on a narrow set of examples, instead of exposure to a broad and varied range of material.

Similarly, she expects diversity in training data to improve the learning process and points to early – though still preliminary – evidence that better and more diverse datasets may allow models to learn more effectively with less data.

From this perspective, a related goal of the project is to support the creation of better datasets from the outset. “What often happens nowadays is that, once a model's released, we discover all kinds of problems.”

“But,” she argues, “it all starts with the data. If we can understand the data and its influence on model behavior better, we could intervene earlier, before the training and deployment phases.”

At present, however, many decisions about how datasets are constructed and adjusted are still guided by heuristics and remain an “ad hoc” practice. This is partly due to constraints in data availability, but also because, as Nguyen notes, it is difficult to investigate these questions systematically without concrete frameworks or tools to measure diversity in the first place.

--- Challenges ---

Nguyen points to several challenges when attempting to measure data diversity in practice. A first difficulty is conceptual: what actually constitutes a good diversity metric is not always straightforward. “There isn’t just one definition, is there? It really depends on the specific application.”

A second challenge concerns scalability. “Many metrics that are theoretically appealing don’t scale well to the large datasets used for LLMs,” she adds, which might call for certain trade-offs between theoretical elegance and practical usability.

Finally, there is the issue of missing information. Many important axes of diversity, such as dialect or demographic representation, are simply not labeled in most large datasets, which are often collected from the web without rich metadata. “While it may be possible to infer some of this information automatically, the absence of reliable annotations makes it harder to develop fine-grained measures of diversity,” Nguyen says. “This is why our project focuses primarily on metrics that don’t rely on metadata."

belief: Designing diverse and well-understood datasets early in the development pipeline is a more effective way to prevent model problems than trying to fix issues after models are released.
source_quote: it all starts with the data. If we can understand the data and its influence on model behavior better, we could intervene earlier, before the training and deployment phases.
inference_reasoning: By contrasting current post-release problem discovery with the claim that interventions should happen earlier at the data stage, the text implies an underlying belief that proactive dataset design is a superior strategy to reactive mitigation.

belief: Environmental harm is a serious public safety and security issue that deserves the same level of attention as more visible forms of crime.
source_quote: Environmental safety, he says, is not activism but an urgent security issue.
inference_reasoning: By contrasting environmental safety with ‘activism’ and calling it an ‘urgent security issue’, the text presupposes that environmental harm belongs in the core domain of public safety, not at the margins of political protest or niche concern.

But these challenges, she argues, aren’t insurmountable. The team is currently at the first stage of the project, which will result in the release of a software library, containing tools and methods that can be used to measure data diversity. The focus will then shift to investigating the impact of data diversity on model behaviour.

“If we can understand that link better, we can create fairer and more robust models that can handle different types of output more effectively,” Nguyen says.

--- AI lessons from the boardroom ---

=== Inside the Police’s AI approach to environmental crime ===

>>> Police innovator Amir Niknam on environmental crime, complexity and the limits of technology <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

Executives are increasingly confronted with AI. Not as a gadget, but as a governance question: what should you use it for, who remains responsible, and what should you not use it for? In the new interview series "AI Lessons from the Boardroom," we speak with people who have to make those decisions in practice. Today: Amir Niknam, senior innovation advisor at the Dutch National Police, about how AI helps him combat environmental crime.

Niknam is not a typical technology executive. He studied psychology, worked in IT for many years and started at the police as a detective. Since last year he has been part of the High Impact Environmental Crimes team, which deals with the most serious and complex environmental cases in the Netherlands and internationally. These range from illegal waste dumping on industrial sites to cases involving international supply chains, permits, chemical substances and major financial interests.

Environmental crimes are close to Niknam’s heart. He even supported the Extinction Rebellion demonstrations against fossil fuel subsidies that blocked the A12 motorway, of course without breaking any rules or hindering colleagues on duty. Environmental safety, he says, is not activism but an urgent security issue.

“The damage is less visible than street violence, but the impact on health and living conditions is enormous. Some Dutch cities demonstrably face years shorter life expectancy because of air pollution. And we need to stop that.”

--- Playing legal Champions League ---

Environmental cases are notoriously difficult. A single investigation often touches simultaneously on criminal law, administrative law, permits and European regulations. Money and goods flow through producers, transporters and traders, often across borders.

“Anyone who wants to intervene here has to play in the legal Champions League”, says Niknam. “Take the wrong approach and you’re stuck in endless procedures. And time tends to favor companies with deep pockets and strong legal teams. Complexity can become a shield.”

The bottleneck often appears at the start of a case. There may be a signal or report, but what is the most promising legal route? That first analysis currently takes detectives weeks or even months, while the damage continues.

--- "Al Capone" as a principle ---

This is exactly where Niknam and KickstartAI are focusing with an AI tool currently under development. The project, winner of the National AI Challenge 2025, has internally been nicknamed Al Capone.

The reference is telling: the gangster was not convicted of murder, but of tax evasion.

belief: Legal and regulatory complexity systematically advantages well-resourced companies and obstructs effective enforcement against environmental crime.
source_quote: Complexity can become a shield.
inference_reasoning: Describing complexity as a ‘shield’ in a context where ‘time tends to favor companies with deep pockets and strong legal teams’ implies a belief that intricate legal frameworks and procedures function to protect powerful actors from accountability.
source_text:
But these challenges, she argues, aren’t insurmountable. The team is currently at the first stage of the project, which will result in the release of a software library, containing tools and methods that can be used to measure data diversity. The focus will then shift to investigating the impact of data diversity on model behaviour.

“If we can understand that link better, we can create fairer and more robust models that can handle different types of output more effectively,” Nguyen says.

--- AI lessons from the boardroom ---

=== Inside the Police’s AI approach to environmental crime ===

>>> Police innovator Amir Niknam on environmental crime, complexity and the limits of technology <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

Executives are increasingly confronted with AI. Not as a gadget, but as a governance question: what should you use it for, who remains responsible, and what should you not use it for? In the new interview series "AI Lessons from the Boardroom," we speak with people who have to make those decisions in practice. Today: Amir Niknam, senior innovation advisor at the Dutch National Police, about how AI helps him combat environmental crime.

Niknam is not a typical technology executive. He studied psychology, worked in IT for many years and started at the police as a detective. Since last year he has been part of the High Impact Environmental Crimes team, which deals with the most serious and complex environmental cases in the Netherlands and internationally. These range from illegal waste dumping on industrial sites to cases involving international supply chains, permits, chemical substances and major financial interests.

Environmental crimes are close to Niknam’s heart. He even supported the Extinction Rebellion demonstrations against fossil fuel subsidies that blocked the A12 motorway, of course without breaking any rules or hindering colleagues on duty. Environmental safety, he says, is not activism but an urgent security issue.

“The damage is less visible than street violence, but the impact on health and living conditions is enormous. Some Dutch cities demonstrably face years shorter life expectancy because of air pollution. And we need to stop that.”

--- Playing legal Champions League ---

Environmental cases are notoriously difficult. A single investigation often touches simultaneously on criminal law, administrative law, permits and European regulations. Money and goods flow through producers, transporters and traders, often across borders.

“Anyone who wants to intervene here has to play in the legal Champions League”, says Niknam. “Take the wrong approach and you’re stuck in endless procedures. And time tends to favor companies with deep pockets and strong legal teams. Complexity can become a shield.”

The bottleneck often appears at the start of a case. There may be a signal or report, but what is the most promising legal route? That first analysis currently takes detectives weeks or even months, while the damage continues.

--- "Al Capone" as a principle ---

This is exactly where Niknam and KickstartAI are focusing with an AI tool currently under development. The project, winner of the National AI Challenge 2025, has internally been nicknamed Al Capone.

The reference is telling: the gangster was not convicted of murder, but of tax evasion.

belief: Human oversight is considered essential for legitimate and trustworthy use of AI in public institutions like the police.
source_quote: “We operate in a constitutional state. That requires explainability, proportionality and human judgment.”
inference_reasoning: By linking the constitutional state and legitimacy requirements to human judgment, the text implies that AI alone cannot meet these standards and that human oversight is a non‑negotiable condition for trustworthy AI use in such contexts.
source_text:
“You see that principle in environmental cases as well”, Niknam explains. “The most serious violation is not always the easiest to prove. Sometimes it’s more effective to revoke a permit or shut down a company than to wait for a drawn-out criminal trial.”

The AI tool only supports the initial analysis phase. An intelligence officer enters context, a report, an anomaly, a suspicion. The system searches accessible legislation, case law and similar cases, but it does not issue judgments.

“You want to see more quickly where the legal opening might be. After that, it remains human work.”

Niknam stresses that he is not responsible for the overall AI policy of the police. “I’m speaking here in a personal capacity.” Yet the questions he encounters, about responsibility, boundaries and legitimacy, are broader ones. Within the police, AI is not introduced lightly, he says.

“We operate in a constitutional state. That requires explainability, proportionality and human judgment.”

It also means that not everything that is technically possible is automatically desirable.

“The police are vigilant and serve the values of the democratic rule of law. That is our mission, and it must always come first.”

--- The broader lesson ---

This is also the lesson he wants to share with other organizations. “Never let AI become a strategy in itself. It should always be a means.”

In his own work, the starting point was very concrete: the slow start of complex environmental investigations. Not reforming the entire police organization, but addressing one clear bottleneck.

According to him, that’s where things often go wrong. Organizations start with the technology instead of the friction.

“The danger is that new technology ends up reinforcing the very problem you’re trying to solve”, he says.

He also sees how many organizations are still structured linearly, according to the model of the industrial revolution: step one, then step two, then step three. That worked in factories, but in knowledge work it leads to waiting times, handovers and layers of control.

“You end up with gatekeepers everywhere. Another check, another form. And the system gradually clogs up.”

The temptation is to replace those gatekeepers with AI. “But that’s like buying a better mop while the tap is still running.”

--- Building blocks instead of chains ---

According to Niknam, the real intervention lies in the design of the process itself. Why must work constantly be handed over? Why not organize it more asynchronously? He advocates working with modular building blocks, like a house where you can renovate one room without demolishing the entire building.

In such a system, AI is a tool, not a load-bearing wall. “AI must be replaceable. Switchable off when the context changes. If you can’t disconnect it without everything collapsing, you have a problem.

Because if the structure itself is flawed, AI won’t fix it. “It will only make things go wrong faster.”

“Don’t wait because the protocol says so”. Niknam concludes. “Act as soon as you can. And that’s exactly where AI can be a wonderful tool.”

=== Employee Spotlight: Anouk Wolters ===

Meet Anouk Wolters, AI Project Lead at KickstartAI. With a background in Complex Systems Engineering & Management, she brings a strong socio-technical perspective to AI: connecting technology, people, and processes to create impact where it matters.

belief: Organizations should redesign their underlying processes before or alongside adopting AI, because technology layered onto flawed structures will exacerbate existing problems.
source_quote: “The danger is that new technology ends up reinforcing the very problem you’re trying to solve”
inference_reasoning: By warning that technology can reinforce existing problems and emphasizing process design and modularity, the text implies a strategic belief that structural process reform is a prerequisite or parallel requirement for effective AI adoption.
source_text:
“You see that principle in environmental cases as well”, Niknam explains. “The most serious violation is not always the easiest to prove. Sometimes it’s more effective to revoke a permit or shut down a company than to wait for a drawn-out criminal trial.”

The AI tool only supports the initial analysis phase. An intelligence officer enters context, a report, an anomaly, a suspicion. The system searches accessible legislation, case law and similar cases, but it does not issue judgments.

“You want to see more quickly where the legal opening might be. After that, it remains human work.”

Niknam stresses that he is not responsible for the overall AI policy of the police. “I’m speaking here in a personal capacity.” Yet the questions he encounters, about responsibility, boundaries and legitimacy, are broader ones. Within the police, AI is not introduced lightly, he says.

“We operate in a constitutional state. That requires explainability, proportionality and human judgment.”

It also means that not everything that is technically possible is automatically desirable.

“The police are vigilant and serve the values of the democratic rule of law. That is our mission, and it must always come first.”

--- The broader lesson ---

This is also the lesson he wants to share with other organizations. “Never let AI become a strategy in itself. It should always be a means.”

In his own work, the starting point was very concrete: the slow start of complex environmental investigations. Not reforming the entire police organization, but addressing one clear bottleneck.

According to him, that’s where things often go wrong. Organizations start with the technology instead of the friction.

“The danger is that new technology ends up reinforcing the very problem you’re trying to solve”, he says.

He also sees how many organizations are still structured linearly, according to the model of the industrial revolution: step one, then step two, then step three. That worked in factories, but in knowledge work it leads to waiting times, handovers and layers of control.

“You end up with gatekeepers everywhere. Another check, another form. And the system gradually clogs up.”

The temptation is to replace those gatekeepers with AI. “But that’s like buying a better mop while the tap is still running.”

--- Building blocks instead of chains ---

According to Niknam, the real intervention lies in the design of the process itself. Why must work constantly be handed over? Why not organize it more asynchronously? He advocates working with modular building blocks, like a house where you can renovate one room without demolishing the entire building.

In such a system, AI is a tool, not a load-bearing wall. “AI must be replaceable. Switchable off when the context changes. If you can’t disconnect it without everything collapsing, you have a problem.

Because if the structure itself is flawed, AI won’t fix it. “It will only make things go wrong faster.”

“Don’t wait because the protocol says so”. Niknam concludes. “Act as soon as you can. And that’s exactly where AI can be a wonderful tool.”

=== Employee Spotlight: Anouk Wolters ===

Meet Anouk Wolters, AI Project Lead at KickstartAI. With a background in Complex Systems Engineering & Management, she brings a strong socio-technical perspective to AI: connecting technology, people, and processes to create impact where it matters.

belief: AI initiatives are more likely to succeed when end users are deeply involved throughout the design and implementation process.
source_quote: Some days, I’m shadowing end users of the AI products we’re building to better understand their workflows and identify where AI can genuinely help.
inference_reasoning: Framing user shadowing and understanding workflows as a core, recurring part of the role implies a taken‑for‑granted belief that close end‑user involvement is a key mechanism for effective AI solutions.
source_text:
Let’s dive into her journey into the field, what drives her work, and how she helps turn AI into solutions that support the people who use them.

>>> What’s your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I’m an AI Project Lead and I work closely with our partners and our internal engineering team to implement AI solutions.

My role is really varied, which is one of the things I enjoy most about it. Some days, I’m shadowing end users of the AI products we’re building to better understand their workflows and identify where AI can genuinely help. Other times I’m working on roadmaps to get everyone aligned and involved early on. And I spend a lot of time with engineers, figuring out the technical approach and making decisions on how we move forward.

>>> What’s your background? <<<

I studied Complex Systems Engineering and Management at TU Delft, focusing on navigating complex environments with different stakeholders, organizations, and technical challenges.

My interest in AI started during my master’s, when I organized a large conference about the impact of AI on the workforce. That’s when I got curious about what it really takes to create impact with AI, but also how to do that responsibly.

I ended up writing my thesis on introducing a socio-technical perspective into machine learning projects – so not just looking at the technical side, but also on how you embed it in processes, involve the right people, and organize governance.

After that, I worked for a couple of years at a startup building an AI governance platform, and I’ve been at Kickstart AI since November 2025.

>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

What really drives me is making sure AI actually serves the people who end up using it. I find it really motivating when you work closely with end users, understand what would genuinely make their lives easier, and then build something they’re happy with and that truly helps them in their day-to-day work.

That becomes even more meaningful to me when there’s a broader societal purpose behind it beyond a business benefit. A good example is a project I’m currently leading at Leiden University Medical Center, where we’re helping nurses reduce their workload while also improving wound care. Knowing that the work can make a real difference in a setting like healthcare is incredibly rewarding.

And that’s also one of the things I really like about working at KickstartAI. We have a close and knowledgeable team with a very open atmosphere, and at the same time we get to work in environments where there’s real room to create tangible impact with AI.

>>> What do you think is the impact of our projects in the Dutch AI ecosystem? <<<

That’s a big question. I think our impact is really significant, because we work closely with close partners that are major players in Dutch society. Through those collaborations, we work on projects that can have a real impact –  both for their organizations and for society more broadly.

What also makes that impact bigger is that we bring together experts from different partners, each with their own perspectives and expertise. That helps us build better, higher-quality solutions than you would if you worked in isolation.

>>> What qualities do you think an AI lead needs to have? <<<

I think first of all, you need to be able to communicate with a lot of different types of people and explain technical concepts in a way that’s easy to understand.

Another important quality is being able to think in systems – not just the technical system itself, but the broader socio-technical system it is part of. So really being able to connect the dots between the technology, the people, the processes, and the organization, and then translate that into concrete steps to actually bring technical solutions to life!

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

belief: Cross‑organizational collaboration among major societal actors is an effective way to increase the societal impact of AI projects.
source_quote: we work closely with close partners that are major players in Dutch society. Through those collaborations, we work on projects that can have a real impact –  both for their organizations and for society more broadly.
inference_reasoning: The text causally links collaboration with major partners to "real impact" for society, implying an underlying assumption that such collaborative structures are a primary lever for amplifying AI’s societal effects.

=== Employee Spotlight: Nic van Vliet ===

Meet Nic van Vliet, AI Project Lead at KickstartAI. With a background in startups, product operations at Google, and his own AI agency, he now focuses on moving AI projects move from idea to adoption. We spoke with Nic about his path into AI, what motivates him in his work, and where he sees the technology making the biggest difference.

>>> What’s your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I’m an AI Project Lead. My role is about helping move projects forward together with our tech team, our partners, and everyone else involved. So it’s a mix of making sure things keep progressing, aligning the right people, and turning ideas into working products.

Right now, I’m leading three projects: one with ING focused on using GenAI to support financial health, and with NS I’m working on detecting unattended objects at train stations, as well as exploring how AI can help improve train energy consumption.

>>> What’s your background? <<<

My background is quite diverse. I studied organization and venture management in the US, and began my career working with early-stage startups. I returned to grad school to study international development and economics, before eventually joining Google, where I spent several years in product operations. In 2024, I moved back into the startup world and, more recently, ventured into AI and built my own AI agency, before joining KickstartAI.

>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

I think we’re at the beginning of a major technological transition that will impact everyone. What drives me is making sure people are as informed as possible about it, understanding both the strengths and the limitations of AI. Not just to use it in their own work, but also to be able to ask the right questions, whether that’s within their organization, or more broadly in society.

For me, a big part of the work is helping bring that understanding into projects and conversations with partners, so that we adopt the technology in a thoughtful and responsible way, rather than just letting it happen to us.

>>> What has stood out to you most about working at KickstartAI so far? <<<

Definitely the people and the shared sense of purpose! And another thing I really enjoy is our way of working. A good example of that for me is the ING Challenge, which I’m really proud of. We brought together 15 participants from the partner companies and worked together to build a generative AI chatbot prototype for ING focused on financial health –  all in just two weeks!

>>> Where do you see AI making the biggest difference in the future? <<<

Climate change is definitely one area where AI can have a real impact. It’s fascinating how much it can speed up material discovery, for example.  And it’s not hard to imagine how new materials could prove useful for fields like energy, conductivity, or carbon capture. Of course, humans still need to test and apply those in the real world, but the fact that AI can uncover those kinds of possibilities at an unprecedented scale makes a big difference.

>>> What’s your advice to people considering a career in AI? <<<

belief: Broad public understanding of both the strengths and limitations of AI is a necessary condition for its responsible use in work and society.
source_quote: “making sure people are as informed as possible about it, understanding both the strengths and the limitations of AI… to be able to ask the right questions”
inference_reasoning: Framing informed understanding as what “drives” the work and linking it to the ability to “ask the right questions” implies a background belief that widespread, nuanced AI literacy is essential for responsible engagement with the technology.
source_text:
I’m always optimizing my route from A to B. For example, I figured out the fastest route from our office to the lunch canteen, and I was surprised that people who’d been there much longer than me didn’t know about it.

belief: Unfocused, fragmented experimentation with AI is a common organisational failure mode that prevents real business impact.
source_quote: If you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.

source_text:
My biggest advice would be to just jump in and start using it. And by that, I don’t just mean ChatGPT – I mean trying different tools, experimenting with different LLMs, building small things yourself, and seeing how they actually work in practice. For me, that hands-on experimentation made AI feel much less abstract and helped me understand it a lot better. If you’re considering a career in AI, I think the best thing you can do is not just consume it, but actually create with it.

And as for project managers in the field, I think an important quality is being able to see a few steps ahead. The more you collaborate, the more you talk to different people, and the more perspectives you bring together, the better you get at anticipating what’s coming next – and in AI especially, that’s really important.

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

I used to perform in musicals in high school – I even played Tony in West Side Story !

--- AI lessons from the field ---

=== Why focus and technology go hand in hand ===

>>> ING's COO Risk Leon Dusée on AI in risk management, why pilots often stall, and how a promising AI solution such as a financial health assistant could help people sooner <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: where do you apply it, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in pilot phase? In the interview series "AI Lessons from the Field," we speak with leaders who have to make those choices in practice. Today: Leon Dusée, Global Head Chief Operating Officer Risk at ING. “AI can add value in thousands of places, but if you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

When speaking with Leon Dusée about the future of banking, it quickly becomes clear that he is not only focused on the latest technological gadgets. Although, as COO Risk at ING, he is responsible for the security and stability of a global digital infrastructure, his focus lies on a surprisingly human aspect: making deliberate choices.

“AI can add value in thousands of places,” he says matter-of-factly. “But that is exactly the danger. If you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

It is a lesson he learned in practice at the bank, where he started eight years ago as a management trainee and rose to global leadership of the operations connected to risk management. Technological choices become reality faster when the entire organization has the right focus.

“Everyone sees opportunities in AI, and those opportunities are real. But in a large organization, the temptation is strong to experiment in many places at once. Before long, you get a proliferation of pilots, proofs of concept, and isolated ideas that never truly land in the core process. The art is to say: this is where we will invest, and this is where we will not. That sounds simple, but it is not. Because it also means saying no to good ideas, to initiatives that may seem smarter or more efficient in themselves.”

--- Critical thinking ---

belief: Effective AI adoption in large organisations depends more on disciplined prioritisation and governance than on the sheer number of pilots or ideas.
source_quote: The art is to say: this is where we will invest, and this is where we will not.
inference_reasoning: Contrasting selective investment with a “proliferation of pilots” implies that strategic focus and governance, rather than volume of experimentation, are the key levers for successful AI implementation.
source_text:
My biggest advice would be to just jump in and start using it. And by that, I don’t just mean ChatGPT – I mean trying different tools, experimenting with different LLMs, building small things yourself, and seeing how they actually work in practice. For me, that hands-on experimentation made AI feel much less abstract and helped me understand it a lot better. If you’re considering a career in AI, I think the best thing you can do is not just consume it, but actually create with it.

And as for project managers in the field, I think an important quality is being able to see a few steps ahead. The more you collaborate, the more you talk to different people, and the more perspectives you bring together, the better you get at anticipating what’s coming next – and in AI especially, that’s really important.

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

I used to perform in musicals in high school – I even played Tony in West Side Story !
